questions.example_title: Combining Logarithms Using the Product Rule
questions.question_html: Express $\log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) + \log_4 k$ as a single logarithm.
questions.question_text: Express $\log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) + \log_4 k$ as a single logarithm.
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab) \]

Using the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows: \begin{align} \log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) + \log_4 k & = \bigl( \log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) \bigr) + \log_4 k \\[5pt] & = \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2\big) + \log_4 k\\[5pt] \end{align}

Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain \[ \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2\big) + \log_4 k = \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2 k\big). \]

Finally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get \begin{align} \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2 k \big) & = \log_{4}\big(k^2 k t t^2\big)\\[5pt] & = \log_{4}\big(k^{2+1} t^{1+2}\big)\\[5pt] & = \log_{4}\big(k^3 t^3\big). \end{align}

Therefore, \[ \log_{4}(k^2 t) + \log_{4}(t^2) + \log_4(k) = \log_{4}\big(k^3 t^3\big). \]

& = \bigl( \log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) \bigr) + \log_4 k \\[5pt]
& = \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2\big) + \log_4 k\\[5pt]
\end{align}
Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain
\[
\log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2\big) + \log_4 k = \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2 k\big).
\]
Finally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get

\log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2 k \big) & = \log_{4}\big(k^2 k t t^2\big)\\[5pt]

\end{align}

\[
\log_{4}(k^2 t) + \log_{4}(t^2) + \log_4(k) = \log_{4}\big(k^3 t^3\big).
\]

questions.example_title: Combining Logarithms Using the Product Rule
questions.question_html: Express $\log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)$ as a single logarithm.
questions.question_text: Express $\log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)$ as a single logarithm.
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab) \]

Using the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows: \begin{align} \log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) + \log_3(pq) & = \bigl( \log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) \bigr) + \log_3(pq)\\[5pt] & = \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)\\[5pt] \end{align}

Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain \[ \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)= \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2 pq\big). \]

Finally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get \begin{align} \log_{3} \big(p^3 q^2 pq\big) & = \log_{3} \big(p^3p q^2 q \big)\\[5pt] & = \log_{3} \big(p^{3+1} q^{2+1} \big)\\[5pt] & = \log_{3}\big(p^4 q^3\big). \end{align}

Therefore, \[ \log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) + \log_3(pq) = \log_{3}\big(p^4 q^3\big). \]

& = \bigl( \log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) \bigr) + \log_3(pq)\\[5pt]
& = \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)\\[5pt]

Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain

\log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)= \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2 pq\big).

\log_{3} \big(p^3 q^2 pq\big)

\log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) + \log_3(pq) = \log_{3}\big(p^4 q^3\big).

questions.example_title: Combining Logarithms With Coefficients Using the Product Rule
questions.question_html: Express $4\log 2+4\log 4 + 4\log 8$ as a single logarithm.
questions.question_text: Express $4\log 2+4\log 4 + 4\log 8$ as a single logarithm.
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab) \]

If we factor the given expression and use the product rule for logarithms, we get

\begin{align} 4\log 2+4\log 4+4 \log 8 &= 4 \left(\log 2+4\log 4 + \log 8\right) \\[5pt] &= 4\log (2 \cdot 4 \cdot 8) \\[5pt] &= 4\log 64. \end{align}

Therefore, \[ 4\log 2+4\log 4 + 4\log 8 = 4\log 64. \]

&= 4\log (2 \cdot 4 \cdot 8) \\[5pt]
&= 4\log 64.
\end{align}
Therefore,
\[
4\log 2+4\log 4 + 4\log 8 = 4\log 64.
\]

questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab) \]

Using the product rule, the given expression can be rewritten as \begin{align} \log_2(8 p) = \log_2 8 + \log_2 p. \end{align}

Since $8 = 2^3,$ we have \[ \log_2 8 = \log_2\left( 2^3 \right) = 3. \]

Therefore, \begin{align} \log_2(8 p) & = \log_2 8 + \log_2 p\\[5pt] & = 3 + \log_2 p. \end{align}

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations
questions.question_html: If $\ln \left( {3-7x} \right) = 6$, then $x=$
questions.question_text: If $\ln \left( {3-7x} \right) = 6$, then $x=$
questions.explanation_html:
To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln \left( {3-7x} \right) &= 6 \\[3pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln \left( {3-7x} \right)} &=e^{\color{blue}6} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln(3-7x)} = 3-7x.$

Therefore, \begin{align*} 3-7x & = e^6 \\[3pt] - 7x &= e^6 - 3 \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{e^6 - 3}{ - 7} \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{3 -e^6}{7}. \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations
questions.question_html: Find the exact solution to $ \ln \left( {4x} \right) = 2 .$
questions.question_text:
Find the exact solution to $
\ln \left( {4x} \right) = 2
.$
questions.explanation_html:
To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln\left( {4x} \right) &= 2 \\[3pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln\left( {4x} \right)} &=e^{\color{blue}2} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln (4x)} = 4x.$

Therefore,

\begin{align*} 4x &= e^{2} \\[5pt] x &= \dfrac{1}{4}e^{2}\\[5pt] x &= \dfrac{e^{2}}{4}. \end{align*}

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by First Rearranging the Equation
questions.question_html: Find the exact solution to $5\ln (7-2x) +13 = 38.$
questions.question_text: Find the exact solution to $5\ln (7-2x) +13 = 38.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we rearrange our equation to isolate the logarithmic term, as follows:

\begin{align*} 5\ln (7-2x) +13 &= 38 \\[3pt] 5\ln (7-2x) &= 25 \\[3pt] \ln (7-2x) &= 5 \end{align*}

To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln{(7-2x)} &= 5 \\[3pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln{(7-2x)}} &=e^{\color{blue}5} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln (7-2x)} = 7-2x.$

Therefore,

\begin{align*} 7-2x &= e^5 \\[5pt] -2x &= e^5-7 \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{e^5-7}{-2} \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{7-e^5}{2}. \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by First Rearranging the Equation
questions.question_html: Find the exact solution to $2\ln (1-5x) +3 = 9.$
questions.question_text: Find the exact solution to $2\ln (1-5x) +3 = 9.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we rearrange our equation to isolate the logarithmic term, as follows:

\begin{align*} 2\ln (1-5x) +3 &= 9 \\[3pt] 2\ln (1-5x) &= 6 \\[3pt] \ln (1-5x) &= 3 \end{align*}

To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln{(1-5x)} &= 3 \\[3pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln{(1-5x)}} &=e^{\color{blue}3} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln (1-5x)} = 1-5x.$

Therefore,

\begin{align*} 1-5x &= e^3 \\[5pt] -5x &= e^3-1 \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{e^3-1}{-5} \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{1-e^3}{5}. \end{align*}

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by Combining Logarithmic Terms
questions.question_html: Find the values of $x$ such that $\ln(x-1) - 3\ln(x-1) = 2.$
questions.question_text: Find the values of $x$ such that $\ln(x-1) - 3\ln(x-1) = 2.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we simplify our equation by collecting the like logarithmic terms, and isolating the logarithm, as follows:

\begin{align*} \ln(x-1) - 3\ln(x-1) &= 2 \\[5pt] (1-3)\ln(x-1) &=2 \\[5pt] -2\ln(x-1) &= 2 \\[5pt] \ln(x-1) &= -1 \end{align*}

To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln(x-1) &= -1 \\[5pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln(x-1)} &= e^{\color{blue}-1} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln (x-1)} = x-1.$

Therefore,

\begin{align*} x-1 &= e^{-1} \\[5pt] x-1 &= \dfrac{1}{e} \\[5pt] x & = \dfrac{1}{e} + 1. \end{align*}

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by Combining Logarithmic Terms
questions.question_html: If $2\ln{x} + 3\ln{x}=20$, then what is the value of $x?$
questions.question_text: If $2\ln{x} + 3\ln{x}=20$, then what is the value of $x?$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we simplify our equation by collecting the like logarithmic terms, and isolating the logarithm, as follows: \begin{align*} 2\ln{x} + 3\ln{x} &= 20 \\[5pt] (2+3)\ln x &= 20 \\[5pt] 5\ln x &= 20 \\[5pt] \ln x &= \dfrac{20}{5}\\[5pt] \ln x &=4. \end{align*}

To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln x &= 4 \\[5pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln x} &= e^{\color{blue}4} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln x} = x.$

Therefore, $ x = e^{4}. $

questions.example_title: Finding the Axis of Symmetry From the X-Intercepts
questions.question_html: Find the axis of symmetry for the parabola sketched above.
questions.question_text: Find the axis of symmetry for the parabola sketched above.
questions.explanation_html:
From the graph, we deduce that the roots are $x_1=-5$ and $x_2=1.$

To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:

\begin{align*} x &= \dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2} = \dfrac{(-5)+1}{2} = \dfrac{-4}{2} =-2. \end{align*}

Therefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-2.$

questions.explanation_html:
From the equation $y=(x-2)(x+6),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=2$ and $x_2=-6.$

To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:

\begin{align*} x &= \dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\[5pt] &= \dfrac{2+(-6)}{2} \\[5pt] &= -2. \end{align*}

Therefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-2.$

questions.example_title: Finding the Axis of Symmetry From the Parabola’s Equation in Factored Form
questions.question_html: Find the axis of symmetry of the parabola $y= (2x-1)\left(4x+7\right).$
questions.question_text: Find the axis of symmetry of the parabola $y= (2x-1)\left(4x+7\right).$
questions.explanation_html:
From the equation $y= (2x-1)\left(4x+7\right),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=\dfrac{1}{2}$ and $x_2=-\dfrac{7}{4}.$

To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:

\begin{align*} x &= \dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\[5pt] &= \dfrac{\left(\dfrac12 - \dfrac74\right)}{2}\\[5pt] &= \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{2}{4} - \dfrac74\right)}{2}\\[5pt] &= \dfrac{\left( -\dfrac54\right)}{2}\\[5pt] &=-\dfrac{5}{8} \end{align*}

Therefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-\dfrac{5}{8}.$
questions.explanation_text:

\begin{align*}
x
&= \dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\[5pt]
&= \dfrac{\left(\dfrac12 - \dfrac74\right)}{2}\\[5pt]
&= \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{2}{4} - \dfrac74\right)}{2}\\[5pt]
&= \dfrac{\left( -\dfrac54\right)}{2}\\[5pt]
&=-\dfrac{5}{8}
\end{align*}
Therefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-\dfrac{5}{8}.$

questions.example_title: Finding an Unknown Value Given the Axis of Symmetry
questions.question_html: The axis of symmetry of the parabola $y=(x-5k)(x+7k)$ is $x=4.$ Find the value of $k.$
questions.question_text: The axis of symmetry of the parabola $y=(x-5k)(x+7k)$ is $x=4.$ Find the value of $k.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we calculate the axis of symmetry in terms of $k.$ Then, since we know that the axis of symmetry is $x=4,$ we will set our result equal to $4.$

From the equation $y=(x-5k)(x+7k),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=5k$ and $x_2=-7k.$

To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:

\begin{align*} x &= \dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\[5pt] &= \dfrac{5k +(-7k)}{2}\\[5pt] &= \dfrac{-2k}{2}\\[5pt] &= -k \end{align*}

We know that the axis of symmetry is $x=4.$ Therefore, we have \begin{align} -k & = 4\\[5pt] k & = \bbox[4pt,border: 1px solid lightgray]{-4}. \end{align}

questions.question_text: The axis of symmetry of $y=\dfrac 34(x+4k)(x+3k)$ is $x=1.$ The value of $k=$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we calculate the axis of symmetry in terms of $k.$ Then, since we know that the axis of symmetry is $x=1,$ we will set our result equal to $1.$

From the equation $y=\dfrac 34(x+4k)(x+3k),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=-4k$ and $x_2=-3k.$

To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:

\begin{align*} x &= \dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\[5pt] &= \dfrac{(-4k) + (-3k)}{2}\\[5pt] &= \dfrac{-7k}{2}\\[5pt] &=-\dfrac{7k}{2}. \end{align*}

We know that the axis of symmetry is $x=1.$ Therefore, we have

\begin{align*} -\dfrac{7k}{2} &= 1 \\[5pt] k &=-\dfrac{2}{7}. \end{align*}

questions.topic_name: Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin
questions.example_title: Finding the Center and Radius of a Circle Given Its Equation
questions.question_html: What is the center and radius of the circle with the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 256?$
questions.question_text: What is the center and radius of the circle with the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 256?$
questions.explanation_html:
The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \[ x^2 + y^2 = r^2. \]

So, the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 256$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius \begin{align*} r^2 & = 256 \\[5pt] r & = \sqrt{256} \\[5pt] r & = 16. \end{align*}

Note that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.

Therefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is $16.$

questions.question_html: Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $11$ units from the origin?
questions.question_text: Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $11$ units from the origin?
questions.explanation_html:
The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $11$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 11$ centered at the origin, as shown below.

So, the required equation is

\begin{align*} x^2 + y^2 &= r^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 &= 11^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 &= 121. \end{align*}

questions.example_title: Finding the Equation of a Circle Given Endpoints of a Diameter
questions.question_html: Given that the points $X$ and $Y$ with coordinates $(5,1)$ and $(-5,-1),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?
questions.question_text: Given that the points $X$ and $Y$ with coordinates $(5,1)$ and $(-5,-1),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?
questions.explanation_html:
The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is \[ x^2 + y^2 = r^2. \]

First, we calculate the length of the diameter $\overline{XY}\mathbin{:}$ \begin{align*} XY &= \sqrt{{(5-(-5))}^2+{(1-(-1))}^2}\\[5pt] &=\sqrt{{10}^2+2^2}\\[5pt] &=\sqrt{100+4}\\[5pt] &=2\sqrt{26} \end{align*}

Since $\overline{XY}$ is a diameter, the radius is \[ r = \dfrac{XY}{2} =\dfrac {2\sqrt{26}}{2}=\sqrt{26}. \] So, the equation of the circle is

\begin{align*} x^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & = {\left(\sqrt{26}\right)}^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & = 26. \end{align*}
questions.explanation_text:
The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is
\[
x^2 + y^2 = r^2.
\]
First, we calculate the length of the diameter $\overline{XY}\mathbin{:}$
\begin{align*}
XY &= \sqrt{{(5-(-5))}^2+{(1-(-1))}^2}\\[5pt]
&=\sqrt{{10}^2+2^2}\\[5pt]
&=\sqrt{100+4}\\[5pt]
&=2\sqrt{26}
\end{align*}
Since $\overline{XY}$ is a diameter, the radius is
\[
r = \dfrac{XY}{2} =\dfrac {2\sqrt{26}}{2}=\sqrt{26}.
\]
So, the equation of the circle is
\begin{align*}
x^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\[5pt]
x^2 + y^2 & = {\left(\sqrt{26}\right)}^2 \\[5pt]
x^2 + y^2 & = 26.
\end{align*}

The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is \[ x^2 + y^2 = r^2. \]

First, we calculate the length of the diameter $XY\mathbin{:}$ \begin{align*} XY &= \sqrt{ (-4-4)^2+ (1-(-1))^2}\\[5pt] &= \sqrt{ (-8)^2+2^2}\\[5pt] &= \sqrt{64+4}\\[5pt] &= 2\sqrt{17} \end{align*}

Since $\overline{XY}$ is a diameter, the radius is \[ r=\dfrac{XY}{2} = \dfrac {2\sqrt{17}}{2}=\sqrt{17}. \]

So, the equation of the circle is

\begin{align*} x^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & =\left(\sqrt{17}\right)^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & = 17. \end{align*}

questions.example_title: Solving a "Greater Than or Equal To" Inequality Given the Graph of the Corresponding Parabola
questions.question_html: Given the graph above, solve the inequality $(x-1)(x-3) \geq 0.$
questions.question_text: Given the graph above, solve the inequality $(x-1)(x-3) \geq 0.$
questions.explanation_html:
Notice that our inequality

\[ (x-1)(x-3) \geq 0 \]

is a "greater than or equal to" inequality. Therefore, we need to find the values of $x$ for which the parabola

\[ y = (x-1)(x-3) \]

is above the $x$-axis or touches this axis. We mark these values of $x$ in blue in the diagram below.

So, the solution to the inequality is $x \leq 1$ or $x \geq 3.$

questions.topic_name: Creating Rows or Columns Containing Zeros Using Gaussian Elimination
questions.example_title: Using Row Operations to Create a Column of Zeros in a 3x3 Matrix
questions.question_html:
\[A = \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}5 & \color{blue}7 \\ -3 & 1 & -5 \\ 2 & 9 & 7 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B=\begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}5 & \color{blue}7 \\ \color{red}0 & 16 & 16 \\ \color{red}0 & -1 & -7 \end{bmatrix} \]

By applying elementary row operations of the form $R_i:=R_i+kR_1,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the elementary row operations?
questions.question_text:
\[A =
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}5 & \color{blue}7 \\
-3 & 1 & -5 \\
2 & 9 & 7
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
B=\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}5 & \color{blue}7 \\
\color{red}0 & 16 & 16 \\
\color{red}0 & -1 & -7
\end{bmatrix}
\]
By applying elementary row operations of the form $R_i:=R_i+kR_1,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the elementary row operations?
questions.explanation_html:
To transform $A$ into $B,$ we need to make the entries below $a_{11}={\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1}}$ equal to ${\color{red}0}.$

To do this, we can apply the following elementary row operations:

\begin{align*} A &= \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 5 & 7 \\ -3 & 1 & -5 \\ 2 & 9 & 7 \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} R_2:=R_2+3R_1 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 5 & 7 \\ \color{red}0 & 16 & 16 \\ 2 & 9 & 7 \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} R_3:=R_3-2R_1 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 5 & 7 \\ \color{red}0 & 16 & 16 \\ \color{red}0 & -1 & -7 \end{bmatrix} \\[5pt] &= B \end{align*}

Therefore, the elementary row operations are

$\qquad$ $R_2:=R_2+3R_1\quad$ and $\quad R_3:=R_3-2R_1.$
questions.explanation_text:

A
&=
\begin{bmatrix}

-3 & 1 & -5 \\
2 & 9 & 7
\end{bmatrix}
&& \qquad
\begin{array}{l}
R_2:=R_2+3R_1
\end{array} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 5 & 7 \\
\color{red}0 & 16 & 16 \\
2 & 9 & 7
\end{bmatrix}
&& \qquad
\begin{array}{l}
R_3:=R_3-2R_1
\end{array} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 5 & 7 \\
\color{red}0 & 16 & 16 \\
\color{red}0 & -1 & -7
\end{bmatrix} \\[5pt]
&= B
\end{align*}
Therefore, the elementary row operations are
$\qquad$ $R_2:=R_2+3R_1\quad$ and $\quad R_3:=R_3-2R_1.$

By applying elementary row operations of the form $R_i:=R_i+kR_3,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the elementary row operations?
questions.explanation_html:
To transform $A$ into $B,$ we need to make the entries above $a_{32}={\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{2}}$ equal to ${\color{red}0}.$

Therefore, the elementary row operations are

$\qquad$ $R_1:=R_1-2R_3\quad$ and $\quad R_2:=R_2+3R_3.$

questions.explanation_html:
To transform $A$ into $B,$ we need to make entries above and below $a_{33}={\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1}}$ equal to ${\color{red}0}.$

To do this, we can apply the following elementary row operations: \begin{align*} A &= \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 & 3& 7\\ -9 & 2 & 2 & -1\\ \color{blue}-5 & \color{blue}6 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}7\\ 4 & -2 & 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} R_1:=R_1-3R_3 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} 14 & -14 & {\color{red}0} & -14\\ -9 & 2 & 2 & -1\\ \color{blue}-5 & \color{blue}6 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}7\\ 4 & -2 & 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} R_2:=R_2-2R_3 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} 14 & -14 & {\color{red}0} & -14\\ 1 & -10 & {\color{red}0} & -15\\ \color{blue}-5 & \color{blue}6 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}7\\ 4 & -2 & 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} R_4:=R_4-2R_3 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} 14 & -14 & {\color{red}0} & -14\\ 1 & -10 & {\color{red}0} & -15\\ \color{blue}-5 & \color{blue}6 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}7\\ 14 & -14 & {\color{red}0}& -8 \end{bmatrix} \\[5pt] &= B. \end{align*}

Therefore, the missing elementary row operations are

$\qquad$ $R_2 := R_2 - 2R_3\quad$ and $\quad R_4:=R_4-2R_3.$

Therefore, the missing elementary row operations are

$\qquad$ $R_1 := R_1 + 2 R_2\quad$ and $\quad R_4:=R_4+ R_2.$

questions.example_title: Using Column Operations to Create a Row of Zeros in a 3x3 Matrix
questions.question_html:
\[A = \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}9 & -2 & -16 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & 2 & 8 \\ \color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\ \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B=\begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}9 & 7 & 20 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 \\ \color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\ \end{bmatrix} \]

By applying elementary column operations of the form $C_i:=C_i+kC_1,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the elementary column operations?
questions.question_text:
\[A =
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}9 & -2 & -16 \\
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & 2 & 8 \\
\color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
B=\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}9 & 7 & 20 \\
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 \\
\color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\
\end{bmatrix}
\]
By applying elementary column operations of the form $C_i:=C_i+kC_1,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the elementary column operations?
questions.explanation_html:
To transform $A$ into $B,$ we need to make entries to the right of $a_{21}={\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2}}$ equal to ${\color{red}0}.$

To do this, we can apply the following elementary column operations:

\begin{align*} A &= \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}9 & -2 & -16 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & 2 & 8 \\ \color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\ \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_2:=C_2+C_1 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}9 & 7 & -16 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & \color{red}0 & 8 \\ \color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\ \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_3:=C_3+4C_1 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}9 & 7 & 20 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 \\ \color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\ \end{bmatrix}\\[5pt] &= B \end{align*}

Therefore, the elementary column operations are

$\qquad$ $C_2:=C_2+C_1\quad$ and $\quad C_3:=C_3+4C_1.$
questions.explanation_text:
To transform $A$ into $B,$ we need to make entries to the right of $a_{21}={\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2}}$ equal to ${\color{red}0}.$

\begin{align*}
A
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}9 & -2 & -16 \\
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & 2 & 8 \\
\color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\
\end{bmatrix}
&& \qquad
\begin{array}{l}
C_2:=C_2+C_1
\end{array} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}9 & 7 & -16 \\
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & \color{red}0 & 8 \\
\color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\
\end{bmatrix}
&& \qquad
\begin{array}{l}
C_3:=C_3+4C_1
\end{array} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}9 & 7 & 20 \\
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{-2} & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 \\
\color{blue}0 & 4 & 2 \\
\end{bmatrix}\\[5pt]
&= B
\end{align*}
Therefore, the elementary column operations are
$\qquad$ $C_2:=C_2+C_1\quad$ and $\quad C_3:=C_3+4C_1.$

questions.example_title: Using Column Operations to Create a Row of Zeros in a 3x3 Matrix
questions.question_html:
\[A = \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}0 & 1 & 2 \\ \color{blue}3 & 7 & 4 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{3} & 6 & 3\\ \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B= \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}0 & 1 & 2 \\ \color{blue}3 & 1 & 1 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{3} & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0\\ \end{bmatrix} \]

By applying elementary column operations of the form $C_i:=C_i+kC_1,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the elementary column operations?
questions.question_text:
\[A =
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}0 & 1 & 2 \\
\color{blue}3 & 7 & 4 \\
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{3} & 6 & 3\\
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
B= \begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}0 & 1 & 2 \\
\color{blue}3 & 1 & 1 \\
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{3} & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0\\
\end{bmatrix}
\]
By applying elementary column operations of the form $C_i:=C_i+kC_1,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the elementary column operations?
questions.explanation_html:
To transform $A$ into $B,$ we need to make entries to the right of $a_{31}={\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{3}}$ equal to ${\color{red}0}.$

To do this, we can apply the following elementary column operations:

\begin{align*} A &= \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}0 & 1 & 2 \\ \color{blue}3 & 7 & 4 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{3} & 6 & 3\\ \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_2:=C_2-2C_1 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}0 & 1 & 2 \\ \color{blue}3 & 1 & 4 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{3} & \color{red}0 & 3\\ \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_3:=C_3-C_1 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}0 & 1 & 2 \\ \color{blue}3 & 1 & 1 \\ \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{3} & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0\\ \end{bmatrix} \\[5pt] &= B \end{align*}

Therefore, the elementary column operations are

$\qquad$ $C_2:=C_2-2C_1\quad$ and $\quad C_3:=C_3-C_1.$

questions.question_html:
\[A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 6 & \color{blue}1 \\ 8 & 1 & 2 & \color{blue}{5} \\ 0 & 9 & -5 & \color{blue}4 \\ 4 & 5 & 3 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B= \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -4 & 3 & \color{blue}1 \\ -12 & -24 & -13 & \color{blue}{5} \\ -16 & -11 & -17 & \color{blue}4 \\ \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} \end{bmatrix} \]

Consider the matrices $A$ and $B$ above. By applying three matrix elementary column operations

  1. $C_1:=C_1-4C_4$
  2. $C_2:= \ldots$
  3. $C_3:= \ldots$

each of the form $C_i:=C_i+kC_4,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the missing elementary column operations?
questions.question_text:
\[A =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 6 & \color{blue}1 \\
8 & 1 & 2 & \color{blue}{5} \\
0 & 9 & -5 & \color{blue}4 \\
4 & 5 & 3 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1}
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
B= \begin{bmatrix}
-3 & -4 & 3 & \color{blue}1 \\
-12 & -24 & -13 & \color{blue}{5} \\
-16 & -11 & -17 & \color{blue}4 \\
\color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1}
\end{bmatrix}
\]
Consider the matrices $A$ and $B$ above. By applying three matrix elementary column operations
$C_1:=C_1-4C_4$
$C_2:= \ldots$
$C_3:= \ldots$
each of the form $C_i:=C_i+kC_4,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the missing elementary column operations?
questions.explanation_html:
To transform $A$ into $B,$ we need to make entries to the left of $a_{44}={\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1}}$ equal to ${\color{red}0}.$

To do this, we can apply the following elementary column operations:

\begin{align*} A &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 6 & \color{blue}1 \\ 8 & 1 & 2 & \color{blue}{5} \\ 0 & 9 & -5 & \color{blue}4 \\ 4 & 5 & 3 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_1:=C_1-4C_4 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 1 & 6 & \color{blue}1 \\ -12 & 1 & 2 & \color{blue}{5} \\ -16 & 9 & -5 & \color{blue}4 \\ \color{red}0 & 5 & 3 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_2:=C_2-5C_4 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -4 & 6 & \color{blue}1 \\ -12 & -24 & 2 & \color{blue}{5} \\ -16 & -11 & -5 & \color{blue}4 \\ \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & 3 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_3:=C_3-3C_4 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -4 & 3 & \color{blue}1 \\ -12 & -24 & -13 & \color{blue}{5} \\ -16 & -11 & -17 & \color{blue}4 \\ \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} \end{bmatrix} \\[5pt] &= B. \end{align*}

Therefore, the corresponding elementary column operations are

$\qquad$ $C_2:=C_2-5C_4\quad$ and $\quad C_3:=C_3-3C_4.$

questions.example_title: Using Column Operations to Create a Row of Zeros in a 4x4 Matrix
questions.question_html:
\[A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & \color{blue}1& 3\\ 3 & 9 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 4\\ 4 & 0 & \color{blue}1 & 7\\ 2 & 3 & \color{blue}0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B= \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -4 & \color{blue}1& -1\\ \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{red}0\\ 1 & -9 & \color{blue}1 & 3\\ 2 & 3 & \color{blue}0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \]

Consider the matrices $A$ and $B$ above. By applying three matrix elementary column operations

  1. $C_1:=C_1 - 3C_3$
  2. $C_2:= \ldots$
  3. $C_4:= \ldots$

each of the form $C_i:=C_i+kC_3,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the missing elementary column operations?
questions.question_text:
\[A =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 5 & \color{blue}1& 3\\
3 & 9 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 4\\
4 & 0 & \color{blue}1 & 7\\
2 & 3 & \color{blue}0 & 1
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
B= \begin{bmatrix}
-2 & -4 & \color{blue}1& -1\\
\color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{red}0\\
1 & -9 & \color{blue}1 & 3\\
2 & 3 & \color{blue}0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\]
Consider the matrices $A$ and $B$ above. By applying three matrix elementary column operations
$C_1:=C_1 - 3C_3$
$C_2:= \ldots$
$C_4:= \ldots$
each of the form $C_i:=C_i+kC_3,$ the matrix $A$ can be transformed into the matrix $B.$ What are the missing elementary column operations?
questions.explanation_html:
To transform $A$ into $B,$ we need to make entries to the left and to the right of $a_{23}={\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1}}$ equal to ${\color{red}0}.$

To do this, we can apply the following elementary column operations:

\begin{align*} A &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & \color{blue}1& 3\\ 3 & 9 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 4\\ 4 & 0 & \color{blue}1 & 7\\ 2 & 3 & \color{blue}0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_1:=C_1-3C_3 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 5 & \color{blue}1& 3\\ \color{red}0 & 9 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 4\\ 1 & 0 & \color{blue}1 & 7\\ 2 & 3 & \color{blue}0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_2:=C_2-9C_3 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -4 & \color{blue}1& 3\\ \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & 4\\ 1 & -9 & \color{blue}1 & 7\\ 2 & 3 & \color{blue}0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} && \qquad \begin{array}{l} C_4:=C_4-4C_3 \end{array} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -4 & \color{blue}1& -1\\ \color{red}0 & \color{red}0 & \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{red}0\\ 1 & -9 & \color{blue}1 & 3\\ 2 & 3 & \color{blue}0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[5pt] &= B. \end{align*}

Therefore, the corresponding elementary column operations are

$\qquad$ $C_2:=C_2-9C_3\quad$ and $\quad C_4:=C_4-4C_3.$

\begin{align*}

&& \qquad

questions.example_title: Calculating a Minor in a 3x3 Matrix With Integer Entries
questions.question_html: Calculate the minor of the entry $b_{22}$ in the matrix $ B= \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 & 13 & -6 \\ 2 & 7 & 0 \\ 5 & 0 & 4 } \right\rbrack. $
questions.question_text:
Calculate the minor of the entry $b_{22}$ in the matrix
$
B= \left\lbrack
\matrix {
3 & 13 & -6 \\
2 & 7 & 0 \\
5 & 0 & 4
}
\right\rbrack.
$
questions.explanation_html:
The minor of $b_{22} = 7$ (second row, second column) is the determinant of the remaining entries when the column and row containing $7$ are removed:

\[ {B} = \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 & {\color{gray}\ast} & -6 \\ {\color{gray}\ast} & \bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{\color{gray}7} & {\color{gray}\ast} \\ 5 & {\color{gray}\ast} & 4 } \right\rbrack \]

So, the minor of $b_{22}=7$ is \begin{align*} \text{det} \left[ \begin{array}{cc} 3 & -6 \\ 5 & 4 \end{array} \right] &= \left| \begin{array}{cc} 3 & -6 \\ 5 & 4 \end{array} \right| \\[5pt] &= 3 \cdot 4 - (-6) \cdot 5 \\[5pt] &= 12 - (-30) \\[5pt] &= 42. \end{align*}
questions.explanation_text:
The minor of $b_{22} = 7$ (second row, second column) is the determinant of the remaining entries when the column and row containing $7$ are removed:
\[
{B} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
3 & {\color{gray}\ast} & -6 \\
{\color{gray}\ast} & \bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{\color{gray}7} & {\color{gray}\ast} \\
5 & {\color{gray}\ast} & 4
}
\right\rbrack
\]
So, the minor of $b_{22}=7$ is
\begin{align*}
\text{det}
\left[
\begin{array}{cc}
3 & -6 \\
5 & 4
\end{array}
\right]
&=
\left|
\begin{array}{cc}
3 & -6 \\
5 & 4
\end{array}
\right| \\[5pt]
&=
3 \cdot 4 - (-6) \cdot 5 \\[5pt]
&= 12 - (-30) \\[5pt]
&= 42.
\end{align*}

questions.example_title: Calculating a Minor in a 3x3 Matrix With Integer Entries
questions.question_html: Calculate the minor of the entry $d_{33}$ in the matrix $ {D} = \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 4 & -1 & 54 \\ 11 & 6 & 7 } \right\rbrack. $
questions.question_text:
Calculate the minor of the entry $d_{33}$ in the matrix
$
{D} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
3 & -2 & 4 \\
4 & -1 & 54 \\
11 & 6 & 7
}
\right\rbrack.
$
questions.explanation_html:
The minor of $d_{33} = 7$ (third row, third column) is the determinant of the remaining entries when the column and row containing $7$ are removed:

\[ {D} = \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 & -2 & {\color{gray}\ast} \\ 4 & -1 & {\color{gray}\ast} \\ {\color{gray}\ast} & {\color{gray}\ast} & \bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{\color{gray}7} } \right\rbrack \]

So, the minor of $d_{33}=7$ is

\begin{align*} \text{det} \left[ \begin{array}{cc} 3 & -2 \\ 4 & -1 \end{array} \right] &= \left| \begin{array}{cc} 3 & -2 \\ 4 & -1 \end{array} \right| \\[5pt] &= 3 \cdot (-1) - (-2) \cdot 4 \\[5pt] &= -3 - (-8) \\[5pt] &= 5. \end{align*}

questions.example_title: Calculating a Minor in a 3x3 Matrix With Variable Entries
questions.question_html: What is the minor of $k_{31}$ in the matrix $ {K} = \left\lbrack \matrix { w & u & u^2 \\ u & w & w^2 \\ 2 & 3 & uw } \right\rbrack ? $
questions.question_text:
What is the minor of $k_{31}$ in the matrix
$
{K} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
w & u & u^2 \\
u & w & w^2 \\
2 & 3 & uw
}
\right\rbrack ?
$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we remove the third row and first column:

\[ {X} = \left\lbrack \matrix { {\color{gray}\ast} & u & u^2 \\ {\color{gray}\ast} & w & w^2 \\ \bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{\color{gray}2} & {\color{gray}\ast}& {\color{gray}\ast} } \right\rbrack \]

Next, we calculate the determinant:

\begin{align*} \text{det} \left[ \begin{array}{cc} u & u^2 \\ w & w^2 \end{array} \right] &= \left|\begin{array}{cc} u & u^2 \\ w & w^2 \end{array}\right| \\[5pt] &= u \cdot w^2 - u^2 \cdot w \\[5pt] &= uw^2 - wu^2\\[5pt] &= uw(w-u) \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Calculating a Minor in a 3x3 Matrix With Variable Entries
questions.question_html: What is the minor of $u_{21}$ in the matrix $ {U} = \left\lbrack \matrix { -4 & j &k-2j \\ kj & j & k \\ 7 & -k & j } \right\rbrack ? $
questions.question_text:
What is the minor of $u_{21}$ in the matrix
$
{U} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-4 & j &k-2j \\
kj & j & k \\
7 & -k & j
}
\right\rbrack ?
$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we remove the second row and the first column:

\[ {U} = \left\lbrack \matrix { {\color{gray}\ast} & j &k-2j \\ {\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{\color{gray}kj}} & {\color{gray}\ast} & {\color{gray}\ast} \\ \color{gray}\ast & -k & j} \right\rbrack \]

Next, we calculate the determinant:

\begin{align*} \require{cancel} \text{det} \left[ \begin{array}{cc} j & k-2j \\ - k & j \end{array} \right] &= \left|\begin{array}{cc} j& k-2j \\ -k & j \end{array}\right| \\[5pt] &= j \cdot j- ( -k)\cdot (k-2j)\\[5pt] &= j^2+k(k-2j ) \\[5pt] &= j^2+k^2 -k2j \\[5pt] &= j^2-2jk + k^2\\[5pt] &= (j-k)^2 \end{align*}
questions.explanation_text:
First, we remove the second row and the first column:
\[
{U} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
{\color{gray}\ast} & j &k-2j \\
{\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{\color{gray}kj}} & {\color{gray}\ast} & {\color{gray}\ast} \\
\color{gray}\ast & -k & j}
\right\rbrack
\]
Next, we calculate the determinant:
\begin{align*}
\require{cancel}
\text{det}
\left[
\begin{array}{cc}
j & k-2j \\
- k & j
\end{array}
\right]
&=
\left|\begin{array}{cc}
j& k-2j \\
-k & j
\end{array}\right| \\[5pt]
&=
j \cdot j- ( -k)\cdot (k-2j)\\[5pt]
&= j^2+k(k-2j ) \\[5pt]
&= j^2+k^2 -k2j \\[5pt]
&= j^2-2jk + k^2\\[5pt]
&= (j-k)^2
\end{align*}

questions.example_title: Solving for an Unknown Given the Value of a Minor
questions.question_html: Given that the minor of $c_{23}$ is $-86$, find all the possible values of $x$ in the matrix \[ C =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -3 & 7&-1\\ 4 & -4& 5\\ 11 &x &6 \end{array}\right]. \]
questions.question_text:
Given that the minor of $c_{23}$ is $-86$, find all the possible values of $x$ in the matrix
\[
C =\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
-3 & 7&-1\\
4 & -4& 5\\
11 &x &6
\end{array}\right].
\]
questions.explanation_html:
First, we remove the second row and third column:

\[ C= \left[\begin{array}{ccc} -3 & 7 & {\color{gray}\ast} \\ {\color{gray}\ast} & {\color{gray}\ast} & \bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{\color{gray}5} \\ 11 & x & {\color{gray}\ast} \end{array}\right] \]

The minor of $c_{23}$ is

\begin{align*} \textrm{det}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -3 & 7\\ 11 & x \end{array} \right] &= \left|\begin{array}{cc} -3 & 7\\ 11 & x \end{array}\right| \\[5pt] &=(-3) \cdot x - 7 \cdot 11 \\[5pt] &=-3x-77. \end{align*}

We're told that the minor of $c_{23}$ is $-86$, so we must have:

\begin{align*} -3x-77 &=-86 \\[3pt] -3x&=-9 \\[3pt] x &= 3 \end{align*}

Therefore, the solution is $x=3.$

11 & x & {\color{gray}\ast}
\end{array}\right]
\]
The minor of $c_{23}$ is
\begin{align*}
\textrm{det}\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-3 & 7\\
11 & x
\end{array} \right]
&= \left|\begin{array}{cc}

11 & x

\begin{align*}
-3x-77 &=-86 \\[3pt]

x &= 3

Therefore, the solution is $x=3.$

questions.example_title: Solving for an Unknown Given the Value of a Minor
questions.question_html: Given that the minor of $m_{22}$ is $3k^2-45$, find all the possible values of $k$ in the matrix \[ M=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 5&1\\ 3 & -4& -1\\ 9k-15 &9 &-3 \end{array}\right]. \]
questions.question_text:
Given that the minor of $m_{22}$ is $3k^2-45$, find all the possible values of $k$ in the matrix
\[
M=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
2 & 5&1\\
3 & -4& -1\\
9k-15 &9 &-3
\end{array}\right].
\]
questions.explanation_html:
First, we remove the second row and second column:

\[ M= \left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & {\color{gray}\ast} & 1 \\ {\color{gray}\ast} & \bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{\color{gray}-4} & {\color{gray}\ast} \\ 9k-15 & {\color{gray}\ast} & -3 \end{array}\right] \]

The minor of $m_{22}$ is

\begin{align*} \textrm{det}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 1\\ 9k-15 &-3 \end{array} \right] &= \left|\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 1\\ 9k-15 &-3 \end{array}\right|\\[5pt] &= 2\cdot (-3) - (9k-15)\cdot 1 \\[5pt] &=-6 - (9k-15)\\[5pt] &= -9k+9. \end{align*}

We're told that the minor of $m_{22}$ is $3k^2-45$, so we must have:

\begin{align*} -9k+9 &=3k^2-45\\[3pt] -3k+3 &=k^2-15\\[3pt] k^2+3k-18 &=0\\[3pt] (k-3)(k+6) &=0 \end{align*}

Therefore, the solutions are $k=3$ and $k=-6.$

questions.example_title: Identifying the Leading Entries of a Matrix
questions.question_html: What is the sum of the leading entries of the $1$st, $2$nd, and $4$th rows in the matrix $A,$ given below? \[ A= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 7 & -2 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 & 1 \\ 4 & 0 & -6 & 9 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \]
questions.question_text:
What is the sum of the leading entries of the $1$st, $2$nd, and $4$th rows in the matrix $A,$ given below?
\[
A=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 7 & -2 \\
0 & -1 & 0 & 1 \\
4 & 0 & -6 & 9 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & -3
\end{bmatrix}
\]
questions.explanation_html:
The first non-zero entry of each row (from left to right) is the leading entry.

Therefore, the leading entries of the $1$st, $2$nd, and $4$th rows of $A$ are $7$, $-1$, and $-3$, respectively.

\[ A =\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{7} & -2 \\ 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-1} & 0 & 1 \\ 4 & 0 & -6 & 9 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-3} \end{bmatrix} \]

So, the answer is $7+(-1)+(-3)=3.$

=\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{7} & -2 \\
0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-1} & 0 & 1 \\
4 & 0 & -6 & 9 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-3}
\end{bmatrix}
\]
So, the answer is $7+(-1)+(-3)=3.$

questions.example_title: Identifying Matrices in Row Echelon Form
questions.question_html:
Which of the following matrices are not in row echelon form?

\[ A= \left[ \begin{array}{cccc} 6 & 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \end{array} \right], \quad B= \left[ \begin{array}{cccccc} 1 & 0 & 5 & 1 & -5 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 4 \end{array} \right], \quad C= \left[ \begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 5 & 3 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 5 & 3 \\ 0 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & -7 & 4 \end{array} \right]. \]
questions.question_text:
Which of the following matrices are not in row echelon form?
\[
A= \left[
\begin{array}{cccc}
6 & 0 & 3 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 3 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\
0 & 0 & 6 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
\right],
\quad
B= \left[
\begin{array}{cccccc}
1 & 0 & 5 & 1 & -5 \\
0 & 0 & 2 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 4
\end{array}
\right],
\quad
C= \left[
\begin{array}{ccc}
5 & 5 & 3 & 4 \\
0 & 1 & 5 & 3 \\
0 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\
0 & 0 & -7 & 4
\end{array}
\right].
\]
questions.explanation_html:
Remember that a matrix is in row echelon form if it satisfies the following conditions:

  1. All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
  2. Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.

With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.

  • The matrix $A$ is not in row echelon form since the leading entry $a_{43} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated). \[ A= \left[\begin{array}{cccc} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{6} & 0 & 3 & 0 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & 1 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{{\color{red}6}} & {\color{blue}0} \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} \end{array}\right] \]

  • The matrix $B$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied). \[ B= \left[\begin{array}{cccccc} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 0 & 5 & 1 & -5 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} & 1 & 0 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{4} \end{array}\right] \]

  • The matrix $C$ is not in row echelon form since the leading entry $c_{32} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated). \[ C=\begin{bmatrix} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{5} & 5 & 3 & 4 \\ {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 5 & 3 \\ {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{{\color{red}2}} & 2 & 2 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-7} & 4 \end{bmatrix} \]

Therefore, only $A$ and $C$ are not in row echelon form.
questions.explanation_text:
Remember that a matrix is in row echelon form if it satisfies the following conditions:
All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.
With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.
The matrix $A$ is not in row echelon form since the leading entry $a_{43} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated).
\[
A= \left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{6} & 0 & 3 & 0 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & 1 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{{\color{red}6}} & {\color{blue}0} \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0}
\end{array}\right]
\]
The matrix $B$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied).
\[
B= \left[\begin{array}{cccccc}
\bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 0 & 5 & 1 & -5 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} & 1 & 0 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{4}
\end{array}\right]
\]
The matrix $C$ is not in row echelon form since the leading entry $c_{32} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated).
\[
C=\begin{bmatrix}
\bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{5} & 5 & 3 & 4 \\
{\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 5 & 3 \\
{\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{{\color{red}2}} & 2 & 2 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-7} & 4
\end{bmatrix}
\]
Therefore, only $A$ and $C$ are not in row echelon form.

Which of the following matrices are not in row echelon form?

\[ A= \left[ \begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array} \right], \quad B= \left[ \begin{array}{ccccc} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array} \right], \quad C= \left[ \begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ \end{array} \right]. \]

0 & 5 & 0 & 4 \\

C= \left[

  1. All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
  2. Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.

With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.

  • The matrix $A$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied). \[ A= \left[\begin{array}{cccc} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 3 & 5 \\ {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 2 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} \end{array}\right] \]

  • The matrix $B$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied). \[ B= \left[\begin{array}{cccccc} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{5} & 0 & 4 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} \end{array}\right] \]

  • The matrix $C$ is not in row echelon form since the leading entry $c_{22} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated). \[ C=\begin{bmatrix} 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} & 3 \\ 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{\color{red}-1} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{ -2} \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ \end{bmatrix} \]

Therefore, only $C$ is not in row echelon form.

questions.example_title: Identifying Augmented Matrices in Row Echelon Form
questions.question_html: Which of the following augmented matrices are in row echelon form? \[ A=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 0 & 9 & 2 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 & 7 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array} \right], \quad B=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 8 & 0 & 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -9 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{array} \right], \quad C=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 0 & -5 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 & 7 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 9 \end{array} \right]. \]
questions.question_text:
Which of the following augmented matrices are in row echelon form?
\[
A=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 0 & 9 & 2 \\
0 & -2 & 0 & 7 \\
0 & 0 & 5 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
\right],
\quad
B=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
8 & 0 & 7 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -9 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}
\right],
\quad
C=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
0 & -5 & 0 & 3 \\
0 & 0 & 6 & 7 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 9
\end{array}
\right].
\]
questions.explanation_html:
Remember that an augmented matrix is in row echelon form if its coefficient matrix satisfies the following conditions:

  1. All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
  2. Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.

With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.

  • The matrix $A$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied). \[ A=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 0 & 9 & 2 \\ {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-2} & 0 & 7 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{5} & 0 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 0 \end{array} \right] \]

  • The matrix $B$ is not in row echelon form since the second row contains only zeros and it is not at the bottom of the matrix (condition I is violated). \[ B=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{9} & 0 & 7 & 0 \\ {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & 0 \\ 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-9} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 0 \end{array} \right] \]

  • The matrix $C$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied). \[ C=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-5} & 0 & 3 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{6} & 7 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 9 \end{array} \right] \]

Therefore, only $A$ and $C$ are in row echelon form.
questions.explanation_text:
Remember that an augmented matrix is in row echelon form if its coefficient matrix satisfies the following conditions:
All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.
With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.
The matrix $A$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied).
\[
A=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
\bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 0 & 9 & 2 \\
{\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-2} & 0 & 7 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{5} & 0 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 0
\end{array}
\right]
\]
The matrix $B$ is not in row echelon form since the second row contains only zeros and it is not at the bottom of the matrix (condition I is violated).
\[
B=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
\bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{9} & 0 & 7 & 0 \\
{\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & 0 \\
0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-9} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 0
\end{array}
\right]
\]
The matrix $C$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied).
\[
C=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
{\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-5} & 0 & 3 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{6} & 7 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 9
\end{array}
\right]
\]
Therefore, only $A$ and $C$ are in row echelon form.

  1. All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
  2. Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.

With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.

  • The matrix $A$ is not in row echelon form since $a_{33} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated). \[ A=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-4} & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{\color{red}6} & 7 \end{array} \right] \]

  • The matrix $B$ is not in row echelon form since the third row contains only zeros and it is not at the bottom of the matrix (condition I is violated). \[ B=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-6} & 3 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{9} & 6 & -1 \\ {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-4} & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array} \right] \]

  • The matrix $C$ is not in row echelon form since $c_{33} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated). \[ C=\left[ \begin{array}{cccc|c} 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & -4 & 5 & 9 \\ 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & -7 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{\color{red}2} & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{5} & 1 \end{array} \right] \]

Therefore, $A,$ $B,$ and $C$ are not in row echelon form.
questions.explanation_text:
Remember that an augmented matrix is in row echelon form if its coefficient matrix satisfies the following conditions:
All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.
With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.
The matrix $A$ is not in row echelon form since $a_{33} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated).
\[
A=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-4} & 1 & 2 \\
0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & -1 \\
0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{\color{red}6} & 7
\end{array}
\right]
\]
The matrix $B$ is not in row echelon form since the third row contains only zeros and it is not at the bottom of the matrix (condition I is violated).
\[
B=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
\bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-6} & 3 & 4 & 0 \\
0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{9} & 6 & -1 \\
{\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-4} & 6 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 5
\end{array}
\right]
\]
The matrix $C$ is not in row echelon form since $c_{33} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated).
\[
C=\left[
\begin{array}{cccc|c}
0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & -4 & 5 & 9 \\
0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & -7 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{\color{red}2} & 0 & -1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{5} & 1
\end{array}
\right]
\]
Therefore, $A,$ $B,$ and $C$ are not in row echelon form.

questions.example_title: Identifying Augmented Matrices in Row Echelon Form
questions.question_html: Which of the following augmented matrices are in row echelon form? \[ A=\left[ \begin{array}{cccc|c} -5 & 0 & 8 & 4 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 & -5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -1 \end{array} \right], \quad B=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 4 & 0 & 6 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 \end{array} \right], \quad C=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 0 & -9 & 0 & -6 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & -7 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array} \right]. \]
questions.question_text:
Which of the following augmented matrices are in row echelon form?
\[
A=\left[
\begin{array}{cccc|c}
-5 & 0 & 8 & 4 & 2 \\
0 & 0 & 3 & -5 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & -2 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -1
\end{array}
\right],
\quad
B=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
4 & 0 & 6 & 8 \\
0 & 0 & 3 & -1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 2
\end{array}
\right],
\quad
C=\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
0 & -9 & 0 & -6 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & -7 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
\right].
\]
questions.explanation_html:
Remember that an augmented matrix is in row echelon form if its coefficient matrix satisfies the following conditions:

  1. All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
  2. Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.

With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.

  • The matrix $A$ is not in row echelon form since $a_{33} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated). \[ A=\left[ \begin{array}{cccc|c} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-5} & 0 & 8 & 4 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & -5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{{\color{red}-2}} & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -1 \end{array} \right] \]

  • The matrix $B$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied). \[ B=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{4} & 0 & 6 & 8 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & -1 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 2 \end{array} \right] \]

  • The matrix $C$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied). \[ C=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{-9} & 0 & -6 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & -7 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 1 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 0 \end{array} \right] \]

Therefore, only $B$ and $C$ are in row echelon form.
questions.explanation_text:
Remember that an augmented matrix is in row echelon form if its coefficient matrix satisfies the following conditions:
All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.
With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.
The matrix $A$ is not in row echelon form since $a_{33} \neq 0$ (condition II is violated).
\[
A=\left[
\begin{array}{cccc|c}

{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 2
\end{array}
\right]

C=\left[

questions.example_title: Identifying Augmented Matrices in Row Echelon Form
questions.question_html:
Which of the following augmented matrices are not in row echelon form?

\[ A= \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 0 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array} \right], \quad B= \left[ \begin{array}{ccccc|c} 5 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 7 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 & 4 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 & 1 \end{array} \right], \quad C= \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 4 \end{array} \right]. \]
questions.question_text:
Which of the following augmented matrices are not in row echelon form?
\[
A= \left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
0 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
\right],
\quad
B= \left[
\begin{array}{ccccc|c}
5 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 7 & 8 \\
0 & 0 & 4 & 4 & 5 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 & 1
\end{array}
\right],
\quad
C= \left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 2 & 4
\end{array}
\right].
\]
questions.explanation_html:
Remember that an augmented matrix is in row echelon form if its coefficient matrix satisfies the following conditions:

  1. All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
  2. Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.

With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.

  • The matrix $A$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied). \[ A= \left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & 4 & 5 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 2 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 0 \end{array}\right] \]

  • The matrix $B$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied). \[ B= \left[\begin{array}{ccccc|c} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{5} & 0 & 1 & 0 & 7 & 8 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{4} & 4 & 5 & 0 \\ {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]. \]

  • The matrix $C$ is not in row echelon form since the second row contains only zeros and it is not at the bottom of the matrix (condition I is violated). \[ C= \left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} & 4 \end{array}\right] \]

Therefore, only $C$ is not in row echelon form.
questions.explanation_text:
Remember that an augmented matrix is in row echelon form if its coefficient matrix satisfies the following conditions:
All the zero rows (rows with only zeros), if any, are placed at the bottom of the matrix.
Each leading entry of a row is in a column to the right of the leading entry of the row above it.
With that in mind, let's look at each matrix in turn.
The matrix $A$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied).
\[
A= \left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
{\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{3} & 4 & 5 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 2 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & 0
\end{array}\right]
\]
The matrix $B$ is in row echelon form (both conditions are satisfied).
\[
B= \left[\begin{array}{ccccc|c}
\bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{5} & 0 & 1 & 0 & 7 & 8 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{4} & 4 & 5 & 0 \\
{\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}0} & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} & 1
\end{array}\right].
\]
The matrix $C$ is not in row echelon form since the second row contains only zeros and it is not at the bottom of the matrix (condition I is violated).
\[
C= \left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
\bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{1} & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
{\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}0} & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \bbox[lightgray, 3pt]{2} & 4
\end{array}\right]
\]
Therefore, only $C$ is not in row echelon form.

questions.example_title: Multiplying a Matrix by a Scalar
questions.question_html: Calculate $\dfrac{1}{3}Q$, if $ {Q} = \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 & 6 \\ 0 & -9 \\ -3 & 0 } \right\rbrack. $
questions.question_text:
Calculate $\dfrac{1}{3}Q$, if
$
{Q} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
3 & 6 \\
0 & -9 \\
-3 & 0
}
\right\rbrack.
$
questions.explanation_html:
We multiply each entry of $Q$ by $\dfrac{1}{3},$ as follows:

\begin{align*} \dfrac{1}{3} {Q} &= \dfrac{1}{3} \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 & 6 \\ 0 & -9 \\ -3 & 0 } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 3 & \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 6 \\ \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 0 & \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot (-9) \\ \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot (-3) & \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 0 } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -3 \\ -1 & 0 } \right\rbrack \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Multiplying a Matrix by a Scalar
questions.question_html: $5 \left\lbrack \matrix { 4 & 1 & -2 } \right\rbrack =$
questions.question_text:
$5
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
4 & 1 & -2
}
\right\rbrack
=$
questions.explanation_html:
We multiply each entry of the matrix by $5$, as follows:

\begin{eqnarray} \eqalign{ 5 \left\lbrack \matrix { 4 & 1 & -2 } \right\rbrack &= \left\lbrack \matrix { 5 \cdot 4 & 5 \cdot 1 & 5 \cdot (-2) } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { 20 & 5 & -10 } \right\rbrack } \end{eqnarray}

questions.example_title: Calculating the Value of a Scalar
questions.question_html: If $k {M} = \left\lbrack \matrix { 0 & 6 & -3 \\ 6 & -9 & 6 \\ 15 & 9 & 0 } \right\rbrack $, where ${M} = \left\lbrack \matrix { 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & -3 & 2 \\ 5 & 3 & 0 } \right\rbrack $ and $k$ is a scalar, calculate the value of $k.$
questions.question_text:
If $k {M} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
0 & 6 & -3 \\
6 & -9 & 6 \\
15 & 9 & 0
}
\right\rbrack
$,
where ${M} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
0 & 2 & -1 \\
2 & -3 & 2 \\
5 & 3 & 0
}
\right\rbrack
$
and $k$ is a scalar, calculate the value of $k.$
questions.explanation_html:
To express $kM,$ we multiply each entry of $M$ by $k,$ as follows:

\begin{align*} k {M} &= k\left\lbrack \matrix { 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & -3 & 2 \\ 5 & 3 & 0 } \right\rbrack\\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { 0 & 2k & -k \\ 2k & -3k & 2k \\ 5k & 3k & 0 } \right\rbrack \end{align*}

Now, we set the above result equal to the matrix given for $kM$ in the problem statement:

\[ \left\lbrack \matrix { 0 & 2k & -k \\ 2k & -3k & 2k \\ 5k & 3k & 0 } \right\rbrack = \left\lbrack \matrix { 0 & 6 & -3 \\ 6 & -9 & 6 \\ 15 & 9 & 0 } \right\rbrack \]

We can solve for $k$ by equating any two corresponding (nonzero) elements. Let's choose the top-right elements, $-k$ and $-3.$ Equating these elements, we get

\begin{align} -k &= -3 \\[3pt] k &= 3. \end{align}

Note: We would still get the same result if we chose any other two corresponding (nonzero) elements.
questions.explanation_text:
To express $kM,$ we multiply each entry of $M$ by $k,$ as follows:
\begin{align*}
k {M} &=
k\left\lbrack
\matrix {
0 & 2 & -1 \\
2 & -3 & 2 \\
5 & 3 & 0
}
\right\rbrack\\[5pt]
&=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
0 & 2k & -k \\
2k & -3k & 2k \\
5k & 3k & 0
}
\right\rbrack
\end{align*}
Now, we set the above result equal to the matrix given for $kM$ in the problem statement:
\[
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
0 & 2k & -k \\
2k & -3k & 2k \\
5k & 3k & 0
}
\right\rbrack
=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
0 & 6 & -3 \\
6 & -9 & 6 \\
15 & 9 & 0
}
\right\rbrack
\]
We can solve for $k$ by equating any two corresponding (nonzero) elements. Let's choose the top-right elements, $-k$ and $-3.$ Equating these elements, we get
\begin{align}
-k &= -3 \\[3pt]
k &= 3.
\end{align}
Note: We would still get the same result if we chose any other two corresponding (nonzero) elements.

questions.example_title: Calculating the Value of a Scalar
questions.question_html: If $k {X} = \left\lbrack \matrix { 2 & 0 \\ -5 & -4 \\ -3 & 2 } \right\rbrack $, where ${X} = \left\lbrack \matrix { -4 & 0 \\ 10 & 8 \\ 6 & -4 } \right\rbrack $ and $k$ is a scalar, calculate the value of $k.$
questions.question_text:
If $k {X} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
2 & 0 \\
-5 & -4 \\
-3 & 2
}
\right\rbrack
$,
where ${X} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-4 & 0 \\
10 & 8 \\
6 & -4
}
\right\rbrack
$
and $k$ is a scalar, calculate the value of $k.$
questions.explanation_html:
To express $kX,$ we multiply each entry of $X$ by $k,$ as follows:

\begin{align*} k{X} &= k \left\lbrack \matrix { -4 & 0 \\ 10 & 8 \\ 6 & -4 } \right\rbrack\\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { -4k & 0 \\ 10k & 8k \\ 6k & -4k } \right\rbrack \end{align*}

Now, we set the above result equal to the matrix given for $kX$ in the problem statement:

\[ \left\lbrack \matrix { -4k & 0 \\ 10k & 8k \\ 6k & -4k } \right\rbrack = \left\lbrack \matrix { 2 & 0 \\ -5 & -4 \\ -3 & 2 } \right\rbrack \]

We can solve for $k$ by equating any two corresponding (nonzero) elements. Let's choose the top-left elements, $-4k$ and $2.$ Equating these elements, we get

\begin{align*} -4k &= 2 \\[5pt] k &= -\dfrac{1}{2}. \end{align*}

Note: We would still get the same result if we chose any other two corresponding (nonzero) elements.
questions.explanation_text:
To express $kX,$ we multiply each entry of $X$ by $k,$ as follows:
\begin{align*}

k \left\lbrack
\matrix {
-4 & 0 \\

}
\right\rbrack\\[5pt]
&=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-4k & 0 \\
10k & 8k \\
6k & -4k
}
\right\rbrack
\end{align*}

\[ \left\lbrack
\matrix {
-4k & 0 \\
10k & 8k \\
6k & -4k
}
\right\rbrack
=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
2 & 0 \\
-5 & -4 \\
-3 & 2
}
\right\rbrack
\]
We can solve for $k$ by equating any two corresponding (nonzero) elements. Let's choose the top-left elements, $-4k$ and $2.$ Equating these elements, we get
\begin{align*}
-4k &= 2 \\[5pt]
k &= -\dfrac{1}{2}.
\end{align*}
Note: We would still get the same result if we chose any other two corresponding (nonzero) elements.

questions.example_title: Calculating the Value of a Scalar
questions.question_html: If $k {L} = \left\lbrack \matrix { -4 & 0 & -10 \\ -6 & -6 & -14 \\ 0 & -10 & -2 } \right\rbrack $, where ${L} = \left\lbrack \matrix { 2 & 0 & 5 \\ 3 & 3 & 7 \\ 0 & 5 & 1 } \right\rbrack $ and $k$ is a scalar, calculate the value of $k.$
questions.question_text:
If $k {L} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-4 & 0 & -10 \\
-6 & -6 & -14 \\
0 & -10 & -2
}
\right\rbrack
$,
where ${L} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
2 & 0 & 5 \\
3 & 3 & 7 \\
0 & 5 & 1
}
\right\rbrack
$
and $k$ is a scalar, calculate the value of $k.$
questions.explanation_html:
To express $kL,$ we multiply each entry of $L$ by $k,$ as follows:

\begin{align*} k {L} &= k\left\lbrack \matrix { 2 & 0 & 5 \\ 3 & 3 & 7 \\ 0 & 5 & 1 } \right\rbrack\\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { 2k & 0 & 5k \\ 3k & 3k & 7k \\ 0 & 5k & k } \right\rbrack \end{align*}

Now, we set the above result equal to the matrix given for $kL$ in the problem statement:

\[ \left\lbrack \matrix { 2k & 0 & 5k \\ 3k & 3k & 7k \\ 0 & 5k & k } \right\rbrack = \left\lbrack \matrix { -4 & 0 & -10 \\ -6 & -6 & -14 \\ 0 & -10 & -2 } \right\rbrack \]

We can solve for $k$ by equating any two corresponding (nonzero) elements. Let's choose the bottom-right elements, $k$ and $-2.$ Equating these elements, we get

\begin{align} k &= -2. \end{align}

Note: We would still get the same result if we chose any other two corresponding (nonzero) elements.
questions.explanation_text:
To express $kL,$ we multiply each entry of $L$ by $k,$ as follows:
\begin{align*}
k {L} &=

\matrix {

0 & 5 & 1
}
\right\rbrack\\[5pt]
&=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {

}
\right\rbrack
\end{align*}
Now, we set the above result equal to the matrix given for $kL$ in the problem statement:
\[
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
2k & 0 & 5k \\
3k & 3k & 7k \\
0 & 5k & k
}
\right\rbrack
=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-4 & 0 & -10 \\

}
\right\rbrack
\]
We can solve for $k$ by equating any two corresponding (nonzero) elements. Let's choose the bottom-right elements, $k$ and $-2.$ Equating these elements, we get
\begin{align}

Note: We would still get the same result if we chose any other two corresponding (nonzero) elements.

questions.example_title: Calculating the Value of a Scalar
questions.question_html: If $k {R} = \left\lbrack \matrix { 2 & -3 \\ 0 & 1 } \right\rbrack $, where $ {R} = \left\lbrack \matrix { -2 & 3 \\ 0 & -1 } \right\rbrack $ and $k$ is a scalar, calculate the value of $k.$
questions.question_text:
If $k {R} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
2 & -3 \\
0 & 1
}
\right\rbrack
$,
where $ {R} =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-2 & 3 \\
0 & -1
}
\right\rbrack
$
and $k$ is a scalar, calculate the value of $k.$
questions.explanation_html:
To express $kR,$ we multiply each entry of $R$ by $k,$ as follows:

\begin{align*} k {R} &= k\left\lbrack \matrix { -2 & 3 \\ 0 & -1 } \right\rbrack\\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { -2k & 3k \\ 0 & -k } \right\rbrack \end{align*}

Now, we set the above result equal to the matrix given for $kR$ in the problem statement:

\[ \left\lbrack \matrix { -2k & 3k \\ 0 & -k } \right\rbrack = \left\lbrack \matrix { 2 & -3 \\ 0 & 1 } \right\rbrack. \]

We can solve for $k$ by equating any two corresponding (nonzero) elements. Let's choose the top-right elements, $3k$ and $-3.$ Equating these elements, we get

\begin{align} 3k &= -3 \\[3pt] k &= -1. \end{align}

Note: We would still get the same result if we chose any other two corresponding (nonzero) elements.

questions.example_title: Calculating a Linear Combination of Two Matrices
questions.question_html: Calculate $-{W} - 2{A}$, where ${W}= \left\lbrack \matrix { -3 \\ 1 \\ 1 } \right\rbrack $ and $ {A}= \left\lbrack \matrix { 5 \\ -2 \\ 2 } \right\rbrack .$
questions.question_text:
Calculate $-{W} - 2{A}$, where
${W}=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-3 \\ 1 \\ 1
}
\right\rbrack
$
and
$
{A}=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
5 \\ -2 \\ 2
}
\right\rbrack
.$
questions.explanation_html:
We multiply each entry of $W$ by $-1$ and each entry of $A$ by $2,$ and then subtract the resulting matrices, as follows:

\[ \begin{align} -{W} - 2{A} &= (-1) \left\lbrack \matrix { -3 \\ 1 \\ 1 } \right\rbrack - 2 \left\lbrack \matrix { 5 \\ -2 \\ 2 } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { (-1) \cdot (-3) \\ (-1) \cdot 1 \\ (-1) \cdot 1 } \right\rbrack - \left\lbrack \matrix { 2 \cdot 5 \\ 2 \cdot (-2) \\ 2 \cdot 2 } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 \\ -1 \\ -1 } \right\rbrack - \left\lbrack \matrix { 10 \\ -4 \\ 4 } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { 3-10 \\ -1-(-4) \\ -1-4 } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { -7 \\ 3 \\ -5 } \right\rbrack \end{align} \]
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Calculating a Linear Combination of Two Matrices
questions.question_html: Calculate $\dfrac{1}{3}{S} - 2{T}$, where ${S}= \left\lbrack \matrix { -3 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 \\ } \right\rbrack $ and $ {T}= \left\lbrack \matrix { 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ } \right\rbrack .$
questions.question_text:
Calculate $\dfrac{1}{3}{S} - 2{T}$, where
${S}=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-3 & -3 \\
-3 & 6 \\
0 & 0 \\
}
\right\rbrack
$
and
$
{T}=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
1 & -2 \\
1 & 0 \\
1 & 1 \\
}
\right\rbrack
.$
questions.explanation_html:
We multiply each entry of $S$ by $\dfrac{1}{3}$ and each entry of $T$ by $2,$ and then subtract the resulting matrices, as follows:

\[ \begin{align} \dfrac{1}{3}{S} - 2{T} &= \dfrac{1}{3} \left\lbrack \matrix { -3 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 \\ } \right\rbrack - 2 \left\lbrack \matrix { 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot (-3) & \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot (-3) \\ \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot (-3) & \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 6 \\ \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 0 & \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 0 \\ } \right\rbrack - \left\lbrack \matrix { 2 \cdot 1 & 2 \cdot (-2) \\ 2 \cdot 1 & 2 \cdot 0 \\ 2 \cdot 1 & 2 \cdot 1 \\ } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 } \right\rbrack - \left\lbrack \matrix { 2 & -4 \\ 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 \\ } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { -1-2 & -1-(-4) \\ -1-2 & 2-0 \\ 0-2 & 0-2 } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { -3 & 3 \\ -3 & 2 \\ -2 & -2 } \right\rbrack \end{align} \]
questions.explanation_text:
We multiply each entry of $S$ by $\dfrac{1}{3}$ and each entry of $T$ by $2,$ and then subtract the resulting matrices, as follows:
\[
\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{3}{S} - 2{T}
&=
\dfrac{1}{3}
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-3 & -3 \\
-3 & 6 \\
0 & 0 \\
}
\right\rbrack
-
2
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
1 & -2 \\
1 & 0 \\
1 & 1 \\
}
\right\rbrack \\[5pt]
&=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
\dfrac{1}{3} \cdot (-3) & \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot (-3) \\
\dfrac{1}{3} \cdot (-3) & \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 6 \\
\dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 0 & \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 0 \\
}
\right\rbrack
-
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
2 \cdot 1 & 2 \cdot (-2) \\
2 \cdot 1 & 2 \cdot 0 \\
2 \cdot 1 & 2 \cdot 1 \\
}
\right\rbrack \\[5pt]
&=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-1 & -1 \\
-1 & 2 \\
0 & 0
}
\right\rbrack
-
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
2 & -4 \\
2 & 0 \\
2 & 2 \\
}
\right\rbrack \\[5pt]
&=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-1-2 & -1-(-4) \\
-1-2 & 2-0 \\
0-2 & 0-2
}
\right\rbrack \\[5pt]
&=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-3 & 3 \\
-3 & 2 \\
-2 & -2
}
\right\rbrack
\end{align}
\]
