questions.example_title: Applying Row Operations To a Coefficients Matrix
questions.question_html: Consider the matrix $C,$ given by \[ C = \begin{bmatrix} 1& 2& 1 \\ -9& 0& -4\\ 2& 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}. \] What matrix results from carrying out the following row operations on $C?$ \[ \begin{align*} R_1 &:= R_1 + 3R_3\\ R_2 &:= R_2 + 5R_3 \end{align*} \]
questions.question_text:
Consider the matrix $C,$ given by
\[
C =
\begin{bmatrix}
1& 2& 1 \\
-9& 0& -4\\
2& 0 & -1
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
What matrix results from carrying out the following row operations on $C?$
\[
\begin{align*}
R_1 &:= R_1 + 3R_3\\
R_2 &:= R_2 + 5R_3
\end{align*}
\]
questions.explanation_html:
Let's list the required operations:

  • The operation $R_1 :=R_1 + 3R_3$ means that we need to add $3$ times the third row to the first row.

  • The operation $R_2 :=R_2 + 5R_3$ means that we need to add $5$ times the third row to the second row.

Applying the operations, we obtain the following:

\[ \begin{align*} C&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ -9 & 0 & -4 \\ {\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}-1} \end{bmatrix} \qquad R_1 := R_1 + {\color{red}3} \cdot {\color{blue}R_3}\\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} 1 + ({\color{red}3})({\color{blue}2}) & 2 + ({\color{red}3})({\color{blue}0}) & 1 + ({\color{red}3})({\color{blue}-1}) \\ -9 & 0 & -4 \\ {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}-1} \end{bmatrix}\\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 2 & -2 \\ -9 & 0 & -4 \\ {\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}-1} \end{bmatrix} \qquad R_2 := R_2 + {\color{red}5} \cdot {\color{blue}R_3}\\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} 7& 2 & -2 \\ -9 + ({\color{red}5})({\color{blue}2}) & 0 + ({\color{red}5})({\color{blue}0}) & -4 + ({\color{red}5})({\color{blue}-1}) \\ {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}-1} \\ \end{bmatrix}\\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 2 & -2 \\ {\color{red}1} & {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}-9} \\ {\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}-1}\\ \end{bmatrix} \end{align*} \]
questions.explanation_text:
Let's list the required operations:
The operation $R_1 :=R_1 + 3R_3$ means that we need to add $3$ times the third row to the first row.
The operation $R_2 :=R_2 + 5R_3$ means that we need to add $5$ times the third row to the second row.
Applying the operations, we obtain the following:
\[
\begin{align*}
C&=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 1 \\
-9 & 0 & -4 \\
{\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}-1}
\end{bmatrix} \qquad R_1 := R_1 + {\color{red}3} \cdot {\color{blue}R_3}\\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
1 + ({\color{red}3})({\color{blue}2}) & 2 + ({\color{red}3})({\color{blue}0}) & 1 + ({\color{red}3})({\color{blue}-1}) \\
-9 & 0 & -4 \\
{\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}-1}
\end{bmatrix}\\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
7 & 2 & -2 \\
-9 & 0 & -4 \\
{\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}-1}
\end{bmatrix} \qquad R_2 := R_2 + {\color{red}5} \cdot {\color{blue}R_3}\\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
7& 2 & -2 \\
-9 + ({\color{red}5})({\color{blue}2}) & 0 + ({\color{red}5})({\color{blue}0}) & -4 + ({\color{red}5})({\color{blue}-1}) \\
{\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}0} & {\color{blue}-1} \\
\end{bmatrix}\\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
7 & 2 & -2 \\
{\color{red}1} & {\color{red}0} & {\color{red}-9} \\
{\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}-1}\\
\end{bmatrix}
\end{align*}
\]
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Applying Row Operations To a Coefficients Matrix
questions.question_html: Consider the matrix $M,$ given by \[ M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 2 \\ -3 & 2 & -1 \\ 6 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}. \] What matrix results from carrying out the row operation $R_1:=R_1 + 4 R_2$ on $M?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the matrix $M,$ given by
\[
M =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & -1 & 2 \\
-3 & 2 & -1 \\
6 & 0 & 4
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
What matrix results from carrying out the row operation $R_1:=R_1 + 4 R_2$ on $M?$
questions.explanation_html:
The operation $R_1:=R_1 + 4 R_2$ means that we need to add $4$ times the second row to the first row.

Applying this row operation, we obtain the following:

\begin{align*} M &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 2 \\ {\color{blue}-3} & {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}-1} \\ 6 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \qquad R_1:=R_1 + {\color{red}4} \cdot {\color{blue}R_2} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} 0 + ({\color{red}4}) ({\color{blue}-3})& -1 + ({\color{red}4}) ({\color{blue}2}) & 2 +({\color{red}4}) ({\color{blue}-1}) \\ {\color{blue}-3}& {\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}-1} \\ 6 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{red}-12 & \color{red}7 & \color{red}-2 \\ {\color{blue}-3}& {\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}-1} \\ 6 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \end{align*}
questions.explanation_text:
The operation $R_1:=R_1 + 4 R_2$ means that we need to add $4$ times the second row to the first row.
Applying this row operation, we obtain the following:
\begin{align*}
M &=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & -1 & 2 \\
{\color{blue}-3} & {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}-1} \\
6 & 0 & 4
\end{bmatrix} \qquad R_1:=R_1 + {\color{red}4} \cdot {\color{blue}R_2} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
0 + ({\color{red}4}) ({\color{blue}-3})& -1 + ({\color{red}4}) ({\color{blue}2}) & 2 +({\color{red}4}) ({\color{blue}-1}) \\
{\color{blue}-3}& {\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}-1} \\
6 & 0 & 4
\end{bmatrix} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{red}-12 & \color{red}7 & \color{red}-2 \\
{\color{blue}-3}& {\color{blue}2}& {\color{blue}-1} \\
6 & 0 & 4
\end{bmatrix}
\end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Applying Row Operations To an Augmented Matrix
questions.question_html: Consider the augmented matrix $M,$ given by \[ M = \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1& 0& 2 & 3 \\ 0& -2& 1 & 4 \\ 1& -1& 1 & 2 \end{array} \right]. \] What matrix results from carrying out the row operation $R_3 := R_3 + (-1) \cdot R_1$ on $M?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the augmented matrix $M,$ given by
\[
M =
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1& 0& 2 & 3 \\
0& -2& 1 & 4 \\
1& -1& 1 & 2
\end{array}
\right].
\]
What matrix results from carrying out the row operation $R_3 := R_3 + (-1) \cdot R_1$ on $M?$
questions.explanation_html:
The operation $R_3 := R_3 + (-1) \cdot R_1$ means that we need to add $-1$ times the first row to the third row.

Applying this row operation, we obtain the following:

\begin{align*} M &= \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} {\color{blue}1}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}3} \\ 0& -2& 1 & 4 \\ 1& -1& 1 & 2 \end{array} \right] \qquad R_3 := R_3 + ({\color{red}-1}) \cdot {\color{blue}R_1} \\[5pt] &\sim \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} {\color{blue}1}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}3} \\ 0& -2& 1 & 4 \\ 1 + ({\color{red}-1})({\color{blue}1})& -1 + ({\color{red}-1})({\color{blue}0})& 1 + ({\color{red}-1})({\color{blue}2}) & 2 + ({\color{red}-1})({\color{blue}3}) \end{array} \right] \\[5pt] &\sim \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} {\color{blue}1}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}3} \\ 0& -2& 1 & 4 \\ \color{red}0& \color{red}-1& \color{red}-1 & \color{red}-1 \end{array} \right] \end{align*}
questions.explanation_text:
The operation $R_3 := R_3 + (-1) \cdot R_1$ means that we need to add $-1$ times the first row to the third row.
Applying this row operation, we obtain the following:
\begin{align*}
M &=
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
{\color{blue}1}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}3} \\
0& -2& 1 & 4 \\
1& -1& 1 & 2
\end{array}
\right] \qquad R_3 := R_3 + ({\color{red}-1}) \cdot {\color{blue}R_1} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
{\color{blue}1}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}3} \\
0& -2& 1 & 4 \\
1 + ({\color{red}-1})({\color{blue}1})& -1 + ({\color{red}-1})({\color{blue}0})& 1 + ({\color{red}-1})({\color{blue}2}) & 2 + ({\color{red}-1})({\color{blue}3})
\end{array}
\right] \\[5pt]
&\sim
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
{\color{blue}1}& {\color{blue}0}& {\color{blue}2} & {\color{blue}3} \\
0& -2& 1 & 4 \\
\color{red}0& \color{red}-1& \color{red}-1 & \color{red}-1
\end{array}
\right]
\end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Applying Row Operations To an Augmented Matrix
questions.question_html:
Consider the augmented matrix $U,$ given by \[ U = \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1& -3& 5 & -2 \\ 3& 9& -8 & 7 \\ 8& 6& 4 & 0 \end{array} \right]. \] What matrix results from carrying out the following row operations on $U?$

\[ \begin{align*} R_2 &:= R_2 + 2R_1 \\[3pt] R_3 &:= -\dfrac12 R_3 \end{align*} \]
questions.question_text:
Consider the augmented matrix $U,$ given by
\[
U =
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1& -3& 5 & -2 \\
3& 9& -8 & 7 \\
8& 6& 4 & 0
\end{array}
\right].
\]
What matrix results from carrying out the following row operations on $U?$
\[
\begin{align*}
R_2 &:= R_2 + 2R_1 \\[3pt]
R_3 &:= -\dfrac12 R_3
\end{align*}
\]
questions.explanation_html:
Let's list the required operations:

  • The operation $R_2 := R_2 + 2R_1$ means that we need to add $2$ times the first row to the second row.

  • The operation $R_3 := -\dfrac{1}{2}R_3$ means that we need to multiply the third row of the matrix by $-\dfrac{1}{2}.$

Applying the operations, we obtain the following:

\begin{align*} U &= \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1& -3& 5 & -2 \\ 3 & 9 & -8 & 7 \\ 8& 6& 4 & 0 \end{array} \right] \qquad R_2 := R_2 + 2 R_1 \\[5pt] &\sim \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1& -3& 5 & -2 \\ 3 + (2)(1) & 9 + (2)(-3) & -8 + (2)(5) & 7 + (2)(-2) \\ 8& 6& 4 & 0 \end{array} \right] \\[5pt] &\sim \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1& -3& 5 & -2 \\ 5 & 3 & 2 & 3 \\ 8& 6& 4 & 0 \end{array} \right] \qquad R_3 := -\dfrac 1 2 \cdot R_3 \\[5pt] &\sim \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 1& -3& 5 & -2 \\ 5 & 3 & 2 & 3 \\ -4 & -3 & -2 & 0 \end{array} \right] \end{align*}
questions.explanation_text:
Let's list the required operations:
The operation $R_2 := R_2 + 2R_1$ means that we need to add $2$ times the first row to the second row.
The operation $R_3 := -\dfrac{1}{2}R_3$ means that we need to multiply the third row of the matrix by $-\dfrac{1}{2}.$
Applying the operations, we obtain the following:
\begin{align*}
U &=
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1& -3& 5 & -2 \\
3 & 9 & -8 & 7 \\
8& 6& 4 & 0
\end{array}
\right] \qquad R_2 := R_2 + 2 R_1 \\[5pt]
&\sim
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1& -3& 5 & -2 \\
3 + (2)(1) & 9 + (2)(-3) & -8 + (2)(5) & 7 + (2)(-2) \\
8& 6& 4 & 0
\end{array}
\right] \\[5pt]
&\sim
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1& -3& 5 & -2 \\
5 & 3 & 2 & 3 \\
8& 6& 4 & 0
\end{array}
\right] \qquad R_3 := -\dfrac 1 2 \cdot R_3 \\[5pt]
&\sim
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1& -3& 5 & -2 \\
5 & 3 & 2 & 3 \\
-4 & -3 & -2 & 0
\end{array}
\right]
\end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Applying Row Operations To an Augmented Matrix
questions.question_html: Consider the augmented matrix $T,$ given by \[ T = \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} 1 & 3& 2\\ -2 & -2& 1 \end{array} \right]. \] What matrix results from carrying out the row operation $R_2 := R_2+2R_1$ on $T?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the augmented matrix $T,$ given by
\[
T =
\left[
\begin{array}{cc|c}
1 & 3& 2\\
-2 & -2& 1
\end{array}
\right].
\]
What matrix results from carrying out the row operation $R_2 := R_2+2R_1$ on $T?$
questions.explanation_html:
The operation $R_2 :=R_2 + 2 R_1$ means that we need to add $2$ times the first row to the second row.

Applying this row operation, we obtain the following:

\begin{align*} T & = \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} \color{blue}1 & \color{blue}3& \color{blue}2\\ -2 & -2& 1\\ \end{array} \right] \qquad R_2 := R_2 + {\color{red}2} \cdot {\color{blue}R_1} \\[5pt] &\sim \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} \color{blue}1 & \color{blue}3 & \color{blue}2\\ -2 + ({\color{red}2})({\color{blue}1}) & -2 + ({\color{red}2})({\color{blue}3})& 1 + ({\color{red}2})({\color{blue}2})\\ \end{array} \right] \\[5pt] &\sim \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} \color{blue}1 & \color{blue} 3& \color{blue}2\\ \color{red}0 & \color{red} 4& \color{red} 5\\ \end{array} \right] \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Applying Row Operations To an Augmented Matrix
questions.question_html: Consider the augmented matrix $B,$ given by \[ B = \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} 1 & -1& 2\\ 3 & -4& 4 \end{array} \right]. \] What matrix results from carrying out the row operation $R_2 := R_2+(-3)\cdot R_1$ on $B?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the augmented matrix $B,$ given by
\[
B =
\left[
\begin{array}{cc|c}
1 & -1& 2\\
3 & -4& 4
\end{array}
\right].
\]
What matrix results from carrying out the row operation $R_2 := R_2+(-3)\cdot R_1$ on $B?$
questions.explanation_html:
The operation $R_2:=R_2 + (-3)\cdot R_1$ means that we need to add $-3$ times the first row to the second row.

Applying this row operation, we obtain the following: \begin{align*} B & = \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} \color{blue}1 & \color{blue}-1& \color{blue}2\\ 3 & -4& 4\\ \end{array} \right] \qquad R_2 := R_2 + ({\color{red}-3}) \cdot {\color{blue}R_1} \\[5pt] &\sim \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} \color{blue}1 & \color{blue}-1 & \color{blue}2\\ 3+ ({\color{red}-3})({\color{blue}1}) & -4+ ({\color{red}-3})({\color{blue}-1})& 4+ ({\color{red}-3})({\color{blue}2})\\ \end{array} \right] \\[5pt] &\sim \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} \color{blue}1 & \color{blue}-1 & \color{blue}2\\ \color{red}0 & \color{red}-1& \color{red}-2\\ \end{array} \right] \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.example_title: Identifying the Appropriate Row Operation To Transform One Matrix Into Another
questions.question_html: Which of the following elementary row operations transforms the matrix $B$ into the matrix $C$, where \[ B= \begin{bmatrix} 4& 3 & 2\\ 1 & 2 & 1\\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1\\ 4 & 3& 2\\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}? \]
questions.question_text:
Which of the following elementary row operations transforms the matrix $B$ into the matrix $C$, where
\[
B=
\begin{bmatrix}
4& 3 & 2\\
1 & 2 & 1\\
0 & -2 & 1
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
C=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 1\\
4 & 3& 2\\
0 & -2 & 1
\end{bmatrix}?
\]
questions.explanation_html:
First, notice that the $3$rd row is the same in both matrices. The only rows that have changed are the $1$st row and the $2$nd row.

By considering the given row operations that change both the $1$st and the $2$nd rows, we notice that

\begin{align*} B &= \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}4& \color{blue}3 &\color{blue} 2\\ \color{red}1 & \color{red}2 & \color{red}1\\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} & {\color{blue}R_1} \leftrightarrow {\color{red}R_2} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{red}1 & \color{red}2 & \color{red}1\\ \color{blue}4& \color{blue}3 &\color{blue} 2\\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[5pt] &= C. \end{align*}

Therefore, the required row operation is

\[ R_1 \leftrightarrow R_2. \]
questions.explanation_text:
First, notice that the $3$rd row is the same in both matrices. The only rows that have changed are the $1$st row and the $2$nd row.
By considering the given row operations that change both the $1$st and the $2$nd rows, we notice that
\begin{align*}
B &=
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}4& \color{blue}3 &\color{blue} 2\\
\color{red}1 & \color{red}2 & \color{red}1\\
0 & -2 & 1
\end{bmatrix} & {\color{blue}R_1} \leftrightarrow {\color{red}R_2} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{red}1 & \color{red}2 & \color{red}1\\
\color{blue}4& \color{blue}3 &\color{blue} 2\\
0 & -2 & 1
\end{bmatrix} \\[5pt]
&= C.
\end{align*}
Therefore, the required row operation is
\[
R_1 \leftrightarrow R_2.
\]

questions.example_title: Identifying the Appropriate Row Operation To Transform One Matrix Into Another
questions.question_html: Which of the following elementary row operations transforms the matrix $U$ into the matrix $V$, where \[ U= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 2\\ 6 & 4 & 5\\ 1 & 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & -1\\ 6 & 4 & 5\\ 1 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}? \]
questions.question_text:
Which of the following elementary row operations transforms the matrix $U$ into the matrix $V$, where
\[
U=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 3 & 2\\
6 & 4 & 5\\
1 & 2 & -1
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
V=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & -1\\
6 & 4 & 5\\
1 & 3 & 2
\end{bmatrix}?
\]
questions.explanation_html:
First, notice that the $2$nd row is the same in both matrices. The only rows that have changed are the $1$st row and the $3$rd row.

By considering the given row operations that change both the $1$st and the $3$rd row, we notice that

\begin{align*} U &= \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}1 & \color{blue}3 & \color{blue}2\\ 6 & 4 & 5\\ \color{red}1 & \color{red}2 & \color{red}-1 \end{bmatrix} & \hspace{-0.2cm} {\color{blue}R_1} \leftrightarrow {\color{red}R_3} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{red}1 & \color{red}2 &\color{red} -1\\ 6 & 4 & 5\\ \color{blue}1 & \color{blue}3 & \color{blue}2 \end{bmatrix} \\[5pt] &= V. \end{align*}

Therefore, the required row operation is

\[ R_1 \leftrightarrow R_3. \]
questions.explanation_text:
First, notice that the $2$nd row is the same in both matrices. The only rows that have changed are the $1$st row and the $3$rd row.
By considering the given row operations that change both the $1$st and the $3$rd row, we notice that
\begin{align*}
U &=
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}1 & \color{blue}3 & \color{blue}2\\
6 & 4 & 5\\
\color{red}1 & \color{red}2 & \color{red}-1
\end{bmatrix} & \hspace{-0.2cm} {\color{blue}R_1} \leftrightarrow {\color{red}R_3} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{red}1 & \color{red}2 &\color{red} -1\\
6 & 4 & 5\\
\color{blue}1 & \color{blue}3 & \color{blue}2
\end{bmatrix} \\[5pt]
&= V.
\end{align*}
Therefore, the required row operation is
\[
R_1 \leftrightarrow R_3.
\]

questions.example_title: Identifying the Appropriate Row Operation To Transform a Given Entry to a Desired Value
questions.question_html: Given $ D = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & -1\\ 0 & 4 & 3\\ 3 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $, which of the following elementary row operations makes $a_{31}=0?$
questions.question_text:
Given
$
D =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 3 & -1\\
0 & 4 & 3\\
3 & 2 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
$,
which of the following elementary row operations makes $a_{31}=0?$
questions.explanation_html:
Of the given options, we consider the row operation

\[ R_3 := R_3 + (-3) R_1. \]

Applying the above row operation, we obtain the following:

\[\begin{align*} D &= \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}1& \color{blue}3& \color{blue}-1\\ 0 & 4 & 3\\ 3 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \qquad R_3 := R_3 + ({\color{red}-3}) \cdot {\color{blue}R_1} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1}& \color{blue}3& \color{blue}-1\\ 0 & 4 & 3\\ \color{red}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{0}& \color{red}-7& \color{red}5\\ \end{bmatrix} \end{align*}\]

Therefore, the required row operation is

\[ R_3 := R_3 + (-3)R_1. \]
questions.explanation_text:
Of the given options, we consider the row operation
\[
R_3 := R_3 + (-3) R_1.
\]
Applying the above row operation, we obtain the following:
\[\begin{align*}
D &=
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}1& \color{blue}3& \color{blue}-1\\
0 & 4 & 3\\
3 & 2 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
\qquad R_3 := R_3 + ({\color{red}-3}) \cdot {\color{blue}R_1} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1}& \color{blue}3& \color{blue}-1\\
0 & 4 & 3\\
\color{red}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{0}& \color{red}-7& \color{red}5\\
\end{bmatrix}
\end{align*}\]
Therefore, the required row operation is
\[
R_3 := R_3 + (-3)R_1.
\]

questions.example_title: Identifying the Appropriate Row Operation To Transform a Given Entry to a Desired Value
questions.question_html: Given that $ W = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & -1 \\ -2 & 3 & -4 \\ -1 & 2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} $, which of the following elementary row operations makes $a_{31}=0?$
questions.question_text:
Given that
$
W =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & -1 \\
-2 & 3 & -4 \\
-1 & 2 & -3
\end{bmatrix}
$,
which of the following elementary row operations makes $a_{31}=0?$
questions.explanation_html:
Of the given options, we consider the row operation

\[ R_3 := R_3 + R_1 = R_3 + 1\cdot R_1 \]

Applying the above row operation, we obtain the following:

\begin{align*} W &= \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}1 & \color{blue}2 & \color{blue}-1 \\ -2 & 3 & -4 \\ -1 & 2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \qquad R_3 := R_3 + {\color{red}1} \cdot {\color{blue}R_1} \\[5pt] &\sim \begin{bmatrix} \color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}2 & \color{blue}-1 \\ -2 & 3 & -4 \\ \color{red}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{0} & \color{red}4 & \color{red}-4 \\ \end{bmatrix} \end{align*}

Therefore, the required row operation is

\[ R_3 := R_3 + 1 \cdot R_1 \]

or simply

\[ R_3 := R_3 + R_1. \]
questions.explanation_text:
Of the given options, we consider the row operation
\[
R_3 := R_3 + R_1 = R_3 + 1\cdot R_1
\]
Applying the above row operation, we obtain the following:
\begin{align*}
W &=
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}1 & \color{blue}2 & \color{blue}-1 \\
-2 & 3 & -4 \\
-1 & 2 & -3
\end{bmatrix} \qquad R_3 := R_3 + {\color{red}1} \cdot {\color{blue}R_1} \\[5pt]
&\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
\color{blue}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{1} & \color{blue}2 & \color{blue}-1 \\
-2 & 3 & -4 \\
\color{red}\bbox[lightgray,2pt]{0} & \color{red}4 & \color{red}-4 \\
\end{bmatrix}
\end{align*}
Therefore, the required row operation is
\[
R_3 := R_3 + 1 \cdot R_1
\]
or simply
\[
R_3 := R_3 + R_1.
\]

questions.example_title: Solving a Radical Equation When the Radical Is Not Isolated
questions.question_html: Solve the radical equation $\sqrt{x}+3=10.$
questions.question_text: Solve the radical equation $\sqrt{x}+3=10.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we isolate the $\sqrt{x}$ term by subtracting $3$ from both sides

\begin{align} \sqrt{x}+3 & = 10\\[5pt] \sqrt{x} & = 7 \end{align}

Squaring both sides of the equation, we get \begin{align} \sqrt{x} & = 7\\[3pt] (\sqrt{x})^2 & = 7^2\\[3pt] x & = 49. \end{align}

Let's check the result by plugging it into the original equation: \begin{align} \sqrt{49}+3 & \overset{?}{=} 10\\[5pt] 7+3 & \overset{?}{=} 10\\[5pt] 10 & = 10\;\; {\color{green}\checkmark} \end{align}

The value $x = 49$ satisfies the original equation, so we can accept it as a solution.

Therefore, the solution to $\sqrt{x}+3=10$ is $x = 49.$

questions.example_title: Solving a Radical Equation With an Isolated Radical
questions.question_html: Solve $\sqrt{3x + 4} = 4.$
questions.question_text: Solve $\sqrt{3x + 4} = 4.$
questions.explanation_html:
Squaring both sides of the equation, we get \begin{align} \sqrt{3x + 4} &= 4\\[3pt] \left(\sqrt{3x + 4}\right)^2 & = 4^2\\[3pt] 3x + 4 & = 16\\[3pt] 3x & = 12\\[3pt] x & = 4. \end{align}

Let's check the result by plugging it into the original equation: \begin{align} \sqrt{3\cdot 4 + 4} &\overset{?}{=} 4\\[3pt] \sqrt{12+4} &\overset{?}{=} 4\\[3pt] \sqrt{16} &\overset{?}{=} 4\\[3pt] 4 & = 4\;\; {\color{green}\checkmark} \end{align}

The value $x = 4$ satisfies the original equation, so we can accept it as a solution.

Therefore, the solution to $\sqrt{3x + 4} = 4$ is $x = 4.$
questions.explanation_text:
Squaring both sides of the equation, we get
\begin{align}
\sqrt{3x + 4} &= 4\\[3pt]
\left(\sqrt{3x + 4}\right)^2 & = 4^2\\[3pt]
3x + 4 & = 16\\[3pt]
3x & = 12\\[3pt]
x & = 4.
\end{align}
Let's check the result by plugging it into the original equation:
\begin{align}
\sqrt{3\cdot 4 + 4} &\overset{?}{=} 4\\[3pt]
\sqrt{12+4} &\overset{?}{=} 4\\[3pt]
\sqrt{16} &\overset{?}{=} 4\\[3pt]
4 & = 4\;\; {\color{green}\checkmark}
\end{align}
The value $x = 4$ satisfies the original equation, so we can accept it as a solution.
Therefore, the solution to $\sqrt{3x + 4} = 4$ is $x = 4.$

questions.example_title: Identifying Radical Equations With No Real Solution
questions.question_html:
Which of the following equations have no real solution?

  1. $\sqrt{7x-3} = 5$
  2. $\sqrt{5x+4} =-7$
  3. $\sqrt{7x+1} = -8$
questions.question_text:
Which of the following equations have no real solution?
$\sqrt{7x-3} = 5$
$\sqrt{5x+4} =-7$
$\sqrt{7x+1} = -8$
questions.explanation_html:
Remember that a square root is always positive or zero. So, a radical equation has no real solution if it states that a square root is equal to a negative number.

Of the given equations, only II and III state that a square root is equal to a negative number. Therefore, equations II and III have no real solution.

So, the correct answer is "II and III only".

questions.example_title: Calculating the Second Moment of a Random Variable
questions.question_html:
A random variable $X$ has the probability distribution given below.

$x$ $0$ $1$ $2$ $3$ $4$ $5$ $6$
$f(x)$ $\dfrac 1{10}$ $\dfrac 15$ $\dfrac 25$ $0$ $\dfrac{1}{10}$ $\dfrac{1}{5}$ $0$

Calculate $\textrm E[X^2].$
questions.question_text:
A random variable $X$ has the probability distribution given below.
$x$
$0$
$1$
$2$
$3$
$4$
$5$
$6$
$f(x)$
$\dfrac 1{10}$
$\dfrac 15$
$\dfrac 25$
$0$
$\dfrac{1}{10}$
$\dfrac{1}{5}$
$0$
Calculate $\textrm E[X^2].$
questions.explanation_html:
If $X$ is a discrete random variable with probability mass function $f(x)$ and sample space $S,$ the expected value of $X^2$ is given by \[ \textrm E[X^2] = \sum\limits_{x \in S} x^2 \cdot f(x). \]

Summing up the products of each squared value of $X$ and its associated probability, we get

\begin{align} \textrm E[X^2] &= \sum\limits_{x \in S} x^2 \cdot f(x)\\[5pt] &=0^2\cdot \dfrac{1}{10}+1^2\cdot \dfrac{1}{5}+2^2\cdot \dfrac 25+3^2\cdot 0+4^2\cdot \dfrac {1}{10}+5^2\cdot \dfrac 15+6^2\cdot 0\\[5pt] &=0\cdot \dfrac{1}{10}+1\cdot \dfrac{1}{5}+4\cdot \dfrac 25+9\cdot 0+16\cdot \dfrac {1}{10}+25\cdot \dfrac 15+36\cdot 0\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{42}{5}. \end{align}
questions.explanation_text:
If $X$ is a discrete random variable with probability mass function $f(x)$ and sample space $S,$ the expected value of $X^2$ is given by
\[
\textrm E[X^2] = \sum\limits_{x \in S} x^2 \cdot f(x).
\]
Summing up the products of each squared value of $X$ and its associated probability, we get
\begin{align}
\textrm E[X^2]
&= \sum\limits_{x \in S} x^2 \cdot f(x)\\[5pt]
&=0^2\cdot \dfrac{1}{10}+1^2\cdot \dfrac{1}{5}+2^2\cdot \dfrac 25+3^2\cdot 0+4^2\cdot \dfrac {1}{10}+5^2\cdot \dfrac 15+6^2\cdot 0\\[5pt]
&=0\cdot \dfrac{1}{10}+1\cdot \dfrac{1}{5}+4\cdot \dfrac 25+9\cdot 0+16\cdot \dfrac {1}{10}+25\cdot \dfrac 15+36\cdot 0\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{42}{5}.
\end{align}

questions.example_title: Finding a Difference of Sets Without a Venn Diagram
questions.question_html: If $A = \{a, b, c, d, e\}$ and $B = \{c, d, e, f\},$ then $B - A =$
questions.question_text: If $A = \{a, b, c, d, e\}$ and $B = \{c, d, e, f\},$ then $B - A =$
questions.explanation_html:
The set $B - A$ is the set of all elements belonging to $B$ but not $A.$ Note that the elements $c,d,e \in A$ are also in $B,$ so we must remove them. Therefore, we have

\[ \begin{align*} B - A &= \{ c,d,e,f \} - \{ a,b,c,d,e \} \\[3pt] &= \{ f \}. \end{align*} \]

B - A
&= \{ c,d,e,f \} - \{ a,b,c,d,e \} \\[3pt]
&= \{ f \}.
\end{align*}
\]

questions.example_title: Finding the Product of Two Functions
questions.question_html: Given $f(x) = 3x^2 + 2x$ and $g(x) = 1 - 4x$, find $(f g)(2). $
questions.question_text: Given $f(x) = 3x^2 + 2x$ and $g(x) = 1 - 4x$, find $(f g)(2). $
questions.explanation_html:
The notation $(fg)(x)$ means the product of the functions $f(x)$ and $g(x).$

Method 1

To evaluate $(f g)(2),$ we find $f(2)$ and $g(2),$ and then multiply the results.

First, we find $f(2)$ and $g(2)\mathbin{:}$

\begin{align*} f(2) &=3(2)^2 + 2(2) = 16\\[3pt] g(2) &= 1-4(2) = -7 \end{align*}

Now, we multiply the results:

\[ (fg)(2) = f(2)g(2) = 16\cdot (-7) = -112 \]

Method 2

First, we find $(fg)(x)$ by multiplying the two functions: \[ \eqalign{ (f g)(x) &= (3x^2 + 2x) (1 - 4x) \\[3pt] &= 3x^2 - 12x^3 + 2x - 8x^2\\[3pt] &= -12x^3 - 5x^2 + 2x } \]

Now, we evaluate $(f g)(2)\mathbin{:}$

\[ \eqalign{ (f g)(2) &= -12(2)^3 - 5(2)^2 + 2(2)\\[3pt] &= -96-20+4\\[3pt] &= -112 } \]
questions.explanation_text:
The notation $(fg)(x)$ means the product of the functions $f(x)$ and $g(x).$
Method 1

First, we find $f(2)$ and $g(2)\mathbin{:}$
\begin{align*}
f(2) &=3(2)^2 + 2(2) = 16\\[3pt]
g(2) &= 1-4(2) = -7
\end{align*}
Now, we multiply the results:
\[
(fg)(2) = f(2)g(2) = 16\cdot (-7) = -112
\]
Method 2
First, we find $(fg)(x)$ by multiplying the two functions:
\[
\eqalign{
(f g)(x)
&= (3x^2 + 2x) (1 - 4x) \\[3pt]
&= 3x^2 - 12x^3 + 2x - 8x^2\\[3pt]
&= -12x^3 - 5x^2 + 2x
}
\]
Now, we evaluate $(f g)(2)\mathbin{:}$
\[
\eqalign{
(f g)(2) &= -12(2)^3 - 5(2)^2 + 2(2)\\[3pt]
&= -96-20+4\\[3pt]
&= -112
}
\]

questions.topic_name: The Power and Root Rules for Limits
questions.example_title: Applying the Power Rule to Compute a Limit Given a Graph
questions.question_html: Find $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (x^3+4)^2(4-2f(x))^5}$ for the function $f(x)$ plotted above.
questions.question_text: Find $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (x^3+4)^2(4-2f(x))^5}$ for the function $f(x)$ plotted above.
questions.explanation_html:
From the graph we get that $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} f(x)}=2.$

From the product rule, we have \[ \displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (x^3+4)^2(4-2f(x))^5} = \displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2}(x^3+4)^2}\cdot \displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2}(4-2f(x))^5}. \] We calculate the limits of each factor, separately.

Applying the power rule, we have \begin{align*} \lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (x^3+4)^2 &= \left(\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2}(x^3+4)}\right)^2 \\[5pt] &= \left( (-2)^3+4\right)^2 \\[5pt] &= \left( -8+4\right)^2 \\[5pt] &= 16 \end{align*} and \begin{align*} \lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (4-2f(x))^5 &= \left(\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (4-2f(x))}\right)^5 \\[5pt] &= \left(4-2(2)\right)^5 \\[5pt] &= \left(4-4\right)^5 \\[5pt] &= 0. \end{align*} Finally, we have \begin{align*} \displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (x^3+4)^2(4-2f(x))^5}&= \left( \displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2}(x^3+4)}\right)^2 \left(\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (4-2f(x))}\right)^5\\[5pt] &= 16\cdot 0\\ &=0. \end{align*}
questions.explanation_text:
From the graph we get that $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} f(x)}=2.$
From the product rule, we have
\[
\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (x^3+4)^2(4-2f(x))^5} = \displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2}(x^3+4)^2}\cdot \displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2}(4-2f(x))^5}.
\]
We calculate the limits of each factor, separately.
Applying the power rule, we have
\begin{align*}
\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (x^3+4)^2 &= \left(\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2}(x^3+4)}\right)^2 \\[5pt]
&= \left( (-2)^3+4\right)^2 \\[5pt]
&= \left( -8+4\right)^2 \\[5pt]
&= 16
\end{align*}
and
\begin{align*}
\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (4-2f(x))^5 &= \left(\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (4-2f(x))}\right)^5 \\[5pt]
&= \left(4-2(2)\right)^5 \\[5pt]
&= \left(4-4\right)^5 \\[5pt]
&= 0.
\end{align*}
Finally, we have
\begin{align*}
\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (x^3+4)^2(4-2f(x))^5}&= \left( \displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2}(x^3+4)}\right)^2 \left(\displaystyle{\lim_{x\rightarrow -2} (4-2f(x))}\right)^5\\[5pt]
&= 16\cdot 0\\
&=0.
\end{align*}

questions.example_title: Computing the Terms of a Fibonacci Sequence Given in Sequence Notation
questions.explanation_html:
To compute $a_4,$ we first need to compute $a_3.$ So, we substitute $n=1$ into the recursive formula, as follows:

\[ \eqalign{ a_{n+2} &= a_{n+1} + a_n \\ a_{1+2} & =a_{1+1}+a_1 \\ a_3 & = a_2+a_1 \\ a_3 & = 1+(-1)\\ a_3&= 0 }\]

Next, to compute $a_4,$ we substitute $n=2$ into the recursive formula: \[ \eqalign{ a_{2+2} & =a_{2+1}+a_2 \\ a_4 & = a_3+a_2 \\ a_4 & = 0+1\\ a_4&= 1 }\]

Therefore, $a_4=1.$
questions.explanation_text:
To compute $a_4,$ we first need to compute $a_3.$ So, we substitute $n=1$ into the recursive formula, as follows:
\[
\eqalign{
a_{n+2} &= a_{n+1} + a_n \\
a_{1+2} & =a_{1+1}+a_1 \\
a_3 & = a_2+a_1 \\
a_3 & = 1+(-1)\\
a_3&= 0
}\]
Next, to compute $a_4,$ we substitute $n=2$ into the recursive formula:
\[
\eqalign{
a_{2+2} & =a_{2+1}+a_2 \\
a_4 & = a_3+a_2 \\
a_4 & = 0+1\\
a_4&= 1
}\]
Therefore, $a_4=1.$

questions.example_title: Computing a Probability Over a Bounded Interval: Lower Bound is Not Zero
questions.question_html: Given $X \sim \textrm{Po}(4),$ compute $P(1 \leq X \leq 4),$ rounded to $4$ decimal places.
questions.question_text: Given $X \sim \textrm{Po}(4),$ compute $P(1 \leq X \leq 4),$ rounded to $4$ decimal places.
questions.explanation_html:
If $X \sim \textrm{Po}(\lambda),$ then $X$ has the following probability mass function:

\[ f(x) = \dfrac{\lambda^x e^{-\lambda}}{x!} \]

Here, $X \sim \textrm{Po}(4),$ so the probability distribution of $X$ in this case is

\[ f(x) = \dfrac{4^x e^{-4}}{x!}. \]

Therefore,

\[ \begin{align*} P(1 \leq X \leq 4) &= P(X \in \left\{ 1, 2, 3, 4 \right\}) \\[5pt] &= P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) + P(X = 4) \\[5pt] &= f(1) + f(2) + f(3) + f(4) \\[5pt] &= \dfrac{4^1 e^{-4}}{1!} + \dfrac{4^2 e^{-4}}{2!} + \dfrac{4^3 e^{-4}}{3!} + \dfrac{4^4 e^{-4}}{4!} \\[5pt] &\approx 0.6105. \end{align*} \]
questions.explanation_text:
If $X \sim \textrm{Po}(\lambda),$ then $X$ has the following probability mass function:
\[
f(x) =
\dfrac{\lambda^x e^{-\lambda}}{x!}
\]
Here, $X \sim \textrm{Po}(4),$ so the probability distribution of $X$ in this case is
\[
f(x) =
\dfrac{4^x e^{-4}}{x!}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\begin{align*}
P(1 \leq X \leq 4) &= P(X \in \left\{ 1, 2, 3, 4 \right\}) \\[5pt]
&= P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) + P(X = 3) + P(X = 4) \\[5pt]
&= f(1) + f(2) + f(3) + f(4) \\[5pt]
&= \dfrac{4^1 e^{-4}}{1!} + \dfrac{4^2 e^{-4}}{2!} + \dfrac{4^3 e^{-4}}{3!} + \dfrac{4^4 e^{-4}}{4!} \\[5pt]
&\approx 0.6105.
\end{align*}
\]

questions.example_title: Writing a Product Using Product Notation
questions.question_html: $\dfrac{2 \cdot 2}{3 \cdot 2 + 1} \cdot \dfrac{2 \cdot 3}{3 \cdot 3 + 1} \cdot \dfrac{2 \cdot 4}{3 \cdot 4 + 1} \cdot \: \ldots \: \cdot \dfrac{2p}{3p + 1} =$
questions.question_text: $\dfrac{2 \cdot 2}{3 \cdot 2 + 1} \cdot \dfrac{2 \cdot 3}{3 \cdot 3 + 1} \cdot \dfrac{2 \cdot 4}{3 \cdot 4 + 1} \cdot \: \ldots \: \cdot \dfrac{2p}{3p + 1} =$
questions.explanation_html: We can collapse the given expression using product notation over the index $t$ from $t = 2$ to $t = p{:}$ \begin{align*} \dfrac{2 \cdot \bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{2} }{3 \cdot \bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{2} + 1} \cdot \dfrac{2 \cdot \bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{3} }{3 \cdot \bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{3} + 1} \cdot \dfrac{2 \cdot \bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{4} }{3 \cdot \bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{4} + 1} \cdot \: \ldots \: \cdot \dfrac{2 \bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{p} }{3 \bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{p} + 1} = \prod_{t = 2}^{p} \dfrac{2\bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{t}}{3\bbox[Gainsboro,2pt]{t} + 1} \end{align*}

questions.example_title: Adding Non-Square Matrices
questions.question_html:
Calculate ${M}+ {N},$ where

\[ M = \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 & -6 & 14 \\ 2 & 9 & -13 } \right\rbrack, \quad N= \left\lbrack \matrix { -15 & -6 & -13 \\ -1 & -8 & 14 } \right\rbrack. \]
questions.question_text:
Calculate ${M}+ {N},$ where
\[
M =
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
3 & -6 & 14 \\
2 & 9 & -13
}
\right\rbrack,
\quad
N=
\left\lbrack
\matrix {
-15 & -6 & -13 \\
-1 & -8 & 14
}
\right\rbrack.
\]
questions.explanation_html:
We add the corresponding entries:

\begin{align*} M + N &= \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 & -6 & 14 \\ 2 & 9 & -13 } \right\rbrack + \left\lbrack \matrix { -15 & -6 & -13 \\ -1 & -8 & 14 } \right\rbrack \\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { 3 + (-15) & -6 + (-6) & 14 + (-13) \\ 2 + (-1) & 9 + (-8) & -13 + 14 } \right\rbrack\\[5pt] &= \left\lbrack \matrix { -12 & -12 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 } \right\rbrack \end{align*}

questions.example_title: Solving a Radical Equation With an Isolated Radical
questions.question_text: Solve $\sqrt{3q^2 - 5q + 14} = 4.$
questions.explanation_html:
Squaring both sides of the equation, we get \begin{align} \sqrt{3q^2 - 5q + 14} & = 4\\[5pt] \left( \sqrt{3q^2 - 5q + 14} \right)^2 & = 4^2\\[5pt] 3q^2 - 5q + 14 & = 16\\[5pt] 3q^2 - 5q - 2 & = 0\\[5pt] (3q + 1)(q - 2) & = 0\\[5pt] q & = -\dfrac{1}{3}, \, 2. \end{align}

Let's check the result by plugging the solutions into the original equation.

  • For $q = -\dfrac{1}{3},$ we get \begin{align} \sqrt{3\left(-\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^2 - 5\left(-\dfrac{1}{3}\right) + 14} & \overset{?}{=} 4\\[5pt] \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{3} + \dfrac{5}{3} + 14} & \overset{?}{=} 4\\[5pt] \sqrt{16} &\overset{?}{=} 4\\[5pt] 4 & = 4\;\; {\color{green}\checkmark} \end{align}

  • For $q = 2,$ we get \begin{align} \sqrt{3(2^2) - 5(2) + 14} & \overset{?}{=} 4\\[5pt] \sqrt{12 - 10 + 14} &\overset{?}{=} 4\\[5pt] \sqrt{16} &\overset{?}{=} 4\\[5pt] 4 & = 4\;\; {\color{green}\checkmark} \end{align}

The values $q = -\dfrac{1}{3}, \, 2$ satisfy the original equation, so we can accept them as solutions.

Therefore, the solutions to $\sqrt{3q^2 - 5q + 14} = 4$ are $q = -\dfrac{1}{3}$ and $q = 2.$

questions.example_title: Finding the Complement of a Set Using a Venn Diagram
questions.question_html: Let $U = \{ a, b, c, d, e \}$ be the universal set and let $K = \{ a, c \}.$ What is $\overline{K}?$
questions.question_text: Let $U = \{ a, b, c, d, e \}$ be the universal set and let $K = \{ a, c \}.$ What is $\overline{K}?$
questions.explanation_html:
The complement of $K,$ denoted $\overline K,$ consists of all the elements in $U$ that are not in $K.$ To represent $\overline K,$ we will shade in all the area of $U$ that is outside of $K,$ as follows:

The elements in the shaded area are $b, d,$ and $e.$ These are the elements of $U$ that are not in $K.$ Therefore, the complement of $K$ is

\[ \overline{K} = \{ b, d, e \}. \]

questions.topic_name: The Chain Rule With Logarithmic Functions
questions.example_title: Differentiating a Natural Logarithm Function Using the Chain Rule
questions.question_html: Calculate $\dfrac {\textrm d f}{\textrm d x}$ for $f(x) =5\ln(3-2x)+7.$
questions.question_text: Calculate $\dfrac {\textrm d f}{\textrm d x}$ for $f(x) =5\ln(3-2x)+7.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we express $f(x)=5\ln(3-2x)+7$ as the composite function

\[ f = 5\ln u + 7, \qquad u = 3-2x. \] Differentiating gives

\[ \dfrac{\textrm d f}{\textrm d u} = \dfrac{5}{u},\qquad \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm d x} = -2. \]

We now apply the chain rule, and we get

\[ \eqalign{ \dfrac{\textrm{d}f}{\textrm{d}x}&=\dfrac{\textrm{d}f}{\textrm{d}u}\cdot \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm{d}x}\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{5}{u} \cdot (-2)\\[5pt] &=-\dfrac{10}{u}\\[5pt] &= -\dfrac{10}{3-2x}. } \]
questions.explanation_text:
First, we express $f(x)=5\ln(3-2x)+7$ as the composite function
\[
f = 5\ln u + 7, \qquad u = 3-2x.
\]
Differentiating gives
\[
\dfrac{\textrm d f}{\textrm d u} = \dfrac{5}{u},\qquad \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm d x} = -2.
\]
We now apply the chain rule, and we get
\[
\eqalign{
\dfrac{\textrm{d}f}{\textrm{d}x}&=\dfrac{\textrm{d}f}{\textrm{d}u}\cdot \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm{d}x}\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{5}{u} \cdot (-2)\\[5pt]
&=-\dfrac{10}{u}\\[5pt]
&= -\dfrac{10}{3-2x}.
}
\]

questions.topic_name: The Chain Rule With Logarithmic Functions
questions.example_title: Differentiating a Natural Logarithm Function Using the Chain Rule
questions.question_html: Given that $g(x)=\ln (7x-5)$, find $\dfrac{\textrm{d}g}{\textrm{d}x}.$
questions.question_text: Given that $g(x)=\ln (7x-5)$, find $\dfrac{\textrm{d}g}{\textrm{d}x}.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we express $g(x)$ as the composite function \[ g = \ln u, \qquad u = 7x-5. \]

Differentiating gives

\[ \dfrac{\textrm d g}{\textrm d u} = \dfrac{1}{u}, \qquad \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm d x} = 7. \]

We now apply the chain rule, and we get

\[ \eqalign{ \dfrac{\textrm{d}g}{\textrm{d}x}&=\dfrac{\textrm{d}g}{\textrm{d}u}\cdot \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm{d}x}\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{1}{u} \cdot 7\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{1}{7x-5}\cdot 7\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{7}{7x-5}. } \]
questions.explanation_text:
First, we express $g(x)$ as the composite function
\[
g = \ln u, \qquad u = 7x-5.
\]
Differentiating gives
\[
\dfrac{\textrm d g}{\textrm d u} = \dfrac{1}{u}, \qquad \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm d x} = 7.
\]
We now apply the chain rule, and we get
\[
\eqalign{
\dfrac{\textrm{d}g}{\textrm{d}x}&=\dfrac{\textrm{d}g}{\textrm{d}u}\cdot \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm{d}x}\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{1}{u} \cdot 7\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{1}{7x-5}\cdot 7\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{7}{7x-5}.
}
\]

questions.topic_name: The Chain Rule With Logarithmic Functions
questions.example_title: Differentiating a General Logarithm Function Using the Chain Rule
questions.question_html: Find the equation of the tangent to the curve $y =\log_{2}(3-2x)$ at the point $(1,0).$
questions.question_text: Find the equation of the tangent to the curve $y =\log_{2}(3-2x)$ at the point $(1,0).$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we need to find the slope of the tangent line. The slope is equal to the derivative of the curve at the given point:

\[ m = \dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}\Bigg|_{x=1} \]

To compute $\dfrac{\text dy}{\text dx},$ we first express $y$ as the composite function

\[ y = \log_2{u}, \qquad u = 3-2x. \]

Differentiating gives

\[ \dfrac{\textrm d y}{\textrm d u} = \dfrac{1}{u\ln 2}, \qquad \dfrac{\textrm d u}{\textrm d x} = -2. \]

We now apply the chain rule, and get

\[ \eqalign{ \dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}&=\dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}u}\cdot \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm{d}x}\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{1}{u \ln 2} \cdot (-2)\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{1}{(3-2x)\ln 2}\cdot (-2)\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{-2}{(3-2x)\ln 2}. } \] Therefore, the slope $m$ of the tangent line at the point where $x=1$ is

\begin{eqnarray} \eqalign{ m &= \dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}\Bigg|_{x=1} \\[5pt] &=\dfrac{-2}{(3-2x)\ln 2}\Bigg|_{x=1}\\[5pt] &=-\dfrac{2}{(3-2(1))\ln(2)}\\[5pt] &=-\dfrac{2}{\ln(2)}. } \end{eqnarray}

Now we know that the slope of the tangent line at the point $(1,0)$ is $m=-\dfrac{2}{\ln(2)}.$ Finally, we find the equation of the tangent line at that point:

\begin{align*} y-y_1&=m(x-x_1)\\[5pt] y-0&=-\dfrac{2}{\ln(2)}(x-1)\\[5pt] y&=-\dfrac{2x}{\ln(2)}+\dfrac{2}{\ln(2)}. \end{align*}
questions.explanation_text:
First, we need to find the slope of the tangent line. The slope is equal to the derivative of the curve at the given point:
\[
m = \dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}\Bigg|_{x=1}
\]
To compute $\dfrac{\text dy}{\text dx},$ we first express $y$ as the composite function
\[
y = \log_2{u}, \qquad u = 3-2x.
\]
Differentiating gives
\[
\dfrac{\textrm d y}{\textrm d u} = \dfrac{1}{u\ln 2}, \qquad \dfrac{\textrm d u}{\textrm d x} = -2.
\]
We now apply the chain rule, and get
\[
\eqalign{
\dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}&=\dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}u}\cdot \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm{d}x}\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{1}{u \ln 2} \cdot (-2)\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{1}{(3-2x)\ln 2}\cdot (-2)\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{-2}{(3-2x)\ln 2}.
}
\]
Therefore, the slope $m$ of the tangent line at the point where $x=1$ is
\begin{eqnarray}
\eqalign{
m &= \dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}\Bigg|_{x=1} \\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{-2}{(3-2x)\ln 2}\Bigg|_{x=1}\\[5pt]
&=-\dfrac{2}{(3-2(1))\ln(2)}\\[5pt]
&=-\dfrac{2}{\ln(2)}.
}
\end{eqnarray}
Now we know that the slope of the tangent line at the point $(1,0)$ is $m=-\dfrac{2}{\ln(2)}.$ Finally, we find the equation of the tangent line at that point:
\begin{align*}
y-y_1&=m(x-x_1)\\[5pt]
y-0&=-\dfrac{2}{\ln(2)}(x-1)\\[5pt]
y&=-\dfrac{2x}{\ln(2)}+\dfrac{2}{\ln(2)}.
\end{align*}

questions.topic_name: The Chain Rule With Logarithmic Functions
questions.example_title: Differentiating a General Logarithm Function Using the Chain Rule
questions.question_html: Calculate $\dfrac {\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}$ for $y =\log_3 (\sqrt {2x}).$
questions.question_text: Calculate $\dfrac {\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}$ for $y =\log_3 (\sqrt {2x}).$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we express $y$ as the composite function

\[ y = \log_3 u, \qquad u = \sqrt{2x}. \]

Differentiating gives

\[ \dfrac{\textrm d y}{\textrm d u} = \dfrac{1}{u\ln 3}, \qquad \dfrac{\textrm d u}{\textrm d x} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2}{2\sqrt{x}}= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2x}}. \]

We now apply the chain rule, and get

\[ \eqalign{ \dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}&=\dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}u}\cdot \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm{d}x}\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{1}{u \ln 3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt {2x}}\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt {2x} \ln 3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt {2x}}\\[5pt] &=\dfrac{1}{2x \ln 3}. } \]
questions.explanation_text:
First, we express $y$ as the composite function
\[
y = \log_3 u, \qquad u = \sqrt{2x}.
\]
Differentiating gives
\[
\dfrac{\textrm d y}{\textrm d u} = \dfrac{1}{u\ln 3}, \qquad \dfrac{\textrm d u}{\textrm d x} = \dfrac{\sqrt 2}{2\sqrt{x}}= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2x}}.
\]
We now apply the chain rule, and get
\[
\eqalign{
\dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}x}&=\dfrac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}u}\cdot \dfrac{\textrm{d}u}{\textrm{d}x}\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{1}{u \ln 3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt {2x}}\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt {2x} \ln 3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt {2x}}\\[5pt]
&=\dfrac{1}{2x \ln 3}.
}
\]

questions.topic_name: The Constructive Definition of a Set
questions.example_title: Listing the Elements of a Set Defined Over a Special Set
questions.question_html: Consider the set $A,$ defined as \[ A = \left\{ \dfrac{1}{n^2} : n \in \mathbb {N}\right\}. \] Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the set $A,$ defined as
\[
A = \left\{ \dfrac{1}{n^2} : n \in \mathbb {N}\right\}.
\]
Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$
questions.explanation_html:
The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the sequence $a_n,$ given by \[ a_n = \dfrac{1}{n^2}, \qquad n \in \mathbb N = \{1,2,3,4,\ldots\}. \]

Let's write down the first few terms of our sequence: \begin{align} a_1 & = \dfrac{1}{1^2} = 1\\[5pt] a_2 & = \dfrac{1}{2^2} = \dfrac{1}{4}\\[5pt] a_3 & = \dfrac{1}{3^2} = \dfrac{1}{9}\\[5pt] a_4 & = \dfrac{1}{4^2} = \dfrac{1}{16}\\[5pt] & \vdots \end{align}

Therefore, \[ A = \left\{1,\dfrac{1}{4},\dfrac{1}{9},\dfrac{1}{16},\dfrac{1}{25},\dfrac{1}{36},\dfrac{1}{49}, ...\right\}. \]
questions.explanation_text:
The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the sequence $a_n,$ given by
\[
a_n = \dfrac{1}{n^2}, \qquad n \in \mathbb N = \{1,2,3,4,\ldots\}.
\]
Let's write down the first few terms of our sequence:
\begin{align}
a_1 & = \dfrac{1}{1^2} = 1\\[5pt]
a_2 & = \dfrac{1}{2^2} = \dfrac{1}{4}\\[5pt]
a_3 & = \dfrac{1}{3^2} = \dfrac{1}{9}\\[5pt]
a_4 & = \dfrac{1}{4^2} = \dfrac{1}{16}\\[5pt]
& \vdots
\end{align}
Therefore,
\[
A = \left\{1,\dfrac{1}{4},\dfrac{1}{9},\dfrac{1}{16},\dfrac{1}{25},\dfrac{1}{36},\dfrac{1}{49}, ...\right\}.
\]

questions.topic_name: The Constructive Definition of a Set
questions.example_title: Listing the Elements of a Set Defined Over a Special Set
questions.question_html: Consider the set $A,$ defined as \[ A = \left\{\dfrac{k}{2} : k \in \mathbb{Z}\right\}. \] Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the set $A,$ defined as
\[
A = \left\{\dfrac{k}{2} : k \in \mathbb{Z}\right\}.
\]
Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$
questions.explanation_html:
The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the sequence $a_k,$ given by \[ a_k = \dfrac{k}{2}, \qquad k \in \mathbb Z = \{0, \pm 1,\pm 2,\pm 3,\ldots\}. \]

Let's write down some terms of our sequence until the pattern becomes clear. \begin{align*} & \vdots \\[5pt] a_{-3} & = \dfrac{(-3)}{2} = -\dfrac{3}{2} \\[5pt] a_{-2} & = \dfrac{(-2)}{2} = -1 \\[5pt] a_{-1} & = \dfrac{(-1)}{2} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\\[5pt] a_{0} & = \dfrac{0}{2} = 0\\[5pt] a_{1} & = \dfrac{1}{2}\\[5pt] a_{2} & = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1\\[5pt] a_{3} & = \dfrac{3}{2}\\[5pt] & \vdots \end{align*}

So, the set consists of the elements \[ A = \left\{0, \pm \dfrac{1}{2}, \pm 1, \pm \dfrac{3}{2}, \ldots \right\}. \]
questions.explanation_text:
The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the sequence $a_k,$ given by
\[
a_k = \dfrac{k}{2}, \qquad k \in \mathbb Z = \{0, \pm 1,\pm 2,\pm 3,\ldots\}.
\]
Let's write down some terms of our sequence until the pattern becomes clear.
\begin{align*}
& \vdots \\[5pt]
a_{-3} & = \dfrac{(-3)}{2} = -\dfrac{3}{2} \\[5pt]
a_{-2} & = \dfrac{(-2)}{2} = -1 \\[5pt]
a_{-1} & = \dfrac{(-1)}{2} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\\[5pt]
a_{0} & = \dfrac{0}{2} = 0\\[5pt]
a_{1} & = \dfrac{1}{2}\\[5pt]
a_{2} & = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1\\[5pt]
a_{3} & = \dfrac{3}{2}\\[5pt]
& \vdots
\end{align*}
So, the set consists of the elements
\[
A = \left\{0, \pm \dfrac{1}{2}, \pm 1, \pm \dfrac{3}{2}, \ldots \right\}.
\]

questions.topic_name: The Constructive Definition of a Set
questions.example_title: Listing the Elements of a Set Defined Over a Finite or Infinite Set
questions.question_html:
Consider the set $B,$ defined as \[ B = \left \{ n^3 -4 : n \in \mathbb Z,\, -2 \leq n \leq 2 \right \}. \]

Which of the following sets is equivalent to $B?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the set $B,$ defined as
\[
B = \left \{ n^3 -4 : n \in \mathbb Z,\, -2 \leq n \leq 2 \right \}.
\]
Which of the following sets is equivalent to $B?$
questions.explanation_html:
The condition after the "$:$" symbol \[ n \in \mathbb Z,\, n\leq 4 \]

states that $n$ can be any integer number that satisfies the inequality $-2 \leq n \leq 2.$ In other words, \[ n = \{-2,-1,0,1,2\}. \]

Therefore, the elements of $B$ are all the terms of the sequence $b_n,$ given by \[ b_n = n^3 - 4, \qquad n \in \{-2,-1,0,1,2\}. \]

Let's write down all the terms of our sequence: \begin{align*} b_{-2} &=(-2)^3 -4 = -8-4= -12 \\[5pt] b_{-1} &= (-1)^3 -4 = -1 -4= -5 \\[5pt] b_0 &= 0^3 - 4 = 0 -4 = -4 \\[5pt] b_1 &=1^3 - 4 = 1- 4 = -3 \\[5pt] b_2 &= 2^3 - 4 = 8 - 4= 4 \end{align*}

Therefore, \[ B = \left\{-12,-5,-4,-3,4 \right\}. \]
questions.explanation_text:
The condition after the "$:$" symbol
\[
n \in \mathbb Z,\, n\leq 4
\]
states that $n$ can be any integer number that satisfies the inequality $-2 \leq n \leq 2.$ In other words,
\[
n = \{-2,-1,0,1,2\}.
\]
Therefore, the elements of $B$ are all the terms of the sequence $b_n,$ given by
\[
b_n = n^3 - 4, \qquad n \in \{-2,-1,0,1,2\}.
\]
Let's write down all the terms of our sequence:
\begin{align*}
b_{-2}
&=(-2)^3 -4 = -8-4= -12 \\[5pt]
b_{-1}
&= (-1)^3 -4 = -1 -4= -5 \\[5pt]
b_0
&= 0^3 - 4 = 0 -4 = -4 \\[5pt]
b_1
&=1^3 - 4 = 1- 4 = -3 \\[5pt]
b_2
&= 2^3 - 4 = 8 - 4= 4
\end{align*}
Therefore,
\[
B = \left\{-12,-5,-4,-3,4 \right\}.
\]

questions.example_title: Listing the Elements of a Set Defined Over a Finite or Infinite Set
questions.question_html:
Consider the set $A,$ defined as

\[ A = \left \{ \dfrac{1}{n + 4} : n \in \mathbb{N} \right \}. \]

Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the set $A,$ defined as
\[
A = \left \{ \dfrac{1}{n + 4} : n \in \mathbb{N} \right \}.
\]
Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$
questions.explanation_html:
The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the sequence $a_n,$ given by

\[ a_n = \dfrac{1}{n + 4}, \qquad n \in \mathbb{N}. \]

Let's write down the first few terms of our sequence:

\begin{align*} a_1 &=\dfrac{1}{1+4} =\dfrac{1}{5} \\[5pt] a_2 &= \dfrac{1}{2+4} = \dfrac{1}{6} \\[5pt] a_3 &= \dfrac{1}{3+4} = \dfrac{1}{7} \\[5pt] a_4 &=\dfrac{1}{4+4} = \dfrac{1}{8} \\[5pt] &\vdots \end{align*}

Therefore, \[ A = \left\{\dfrac{1}{5},\dfrac{1}{6},\dfrac{1}{7},\dfrac{1}{8}, \ldots \right\}. \]
questions.explanation_text:
The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the sequence $a_n,$ given by
\[
a_n = \dfrac{1}{n + 4}, \qquad n \in \mathbb{N}.
\]
Let's write down the first few terms of our sequence:
\begin{align*}
a_1
&=\dfrac{1}{1+4} =\dfrac{1}{5} \\[5pt]
a_2
&= \dfrac{1}{2+4} = \dfrac{1}{6} \\[5pt]
a_3
&= \dfrac{1}{3+4} = \dfrac{1}{7} \\[5pt]
a_4
&=\dfrac{1}{4+4} = \dfrac{1}{8} \\[5pt]
&\vdots
\end{align*}
Therefore,
\[
A = \left\{\dfrac{1}{5},\dfrac{1}{6},\dfrac{1}{7},\dfrac{1}{8}, \ldots \right\}.
\]

questions.topic_name: The Constructive Definition of a Set
questions.example_title: Listing the Elements of a Set Defined Using a Recursive Sequence
questions.question_html:
Consider the set $A,$ defined as \[ A = \big \{ a_n\,:\,a_{n+2} = a_{n+1} + 2a_n \,,\, a_1 = 2,\, a_2 =4,\, n \in\mathbb N \big\}. \]

Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the set $A,$ defined as
\[
A = \big \{ a_n\,:\,a_{n+2} = a_{n+1} + 2a_n \,,\, a_1 = 2,\, a_2 =4,\, n \in\mathbb N \big\}.
\]
Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$
questions.explanation_html:
The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the recursive sequence $a_n,$ given by \[ a_{n+2} = a_{n+1} + 2a_n, \quad a_1 = 2,\quad a_2 = 4,\qquad n \in\mathbb N. \]

Let's write down the first few terms of our sequence: \begin{align*} a_1 & = 2\\[10pt] a_2 &=4 \\[10pt] a_3 &= a_2 + 2a_1 \\[5pt] &= 4+2(2)\\[5pt] &=8 \\[10pt] a_4 &= a_3+2a_2\\[5pt] &= 8+2(4)\\[5pt] &=16 \\[10pt] a_5 &= a_4+2a_3\\[5pt] &= 16+2(8)\\[5pt] &=32 \\[10pt] &\vdots \end{align*}

Therefore, \[ A = \left\{2,4,8,16,32, \ldots \right\}. \]

questions.example_title: Listing the Elements of a Set Defined Using a Recursive Sequence
questions.question_html:
Consider the set $B,$ defined as \[ B = \big \{ b_n\,:\,b_{n+1} = 3b_{n} \,,\, b_1 =2,\, n \in \mathbb N \big\}. \]

Which of the following sets is equivalent to $B?$
questions.question_text:
Consider the set $B,$ defined as
\[
B = \big \{ b_n\,:\,b_{n+1} = 3b_{n} \,,\, b_1 =2,\, n \in \mathbb N \big\}.
\]
Which of the following sets is equivalent to $B?$
questions.explanation_html:
The elements of $B$ are all the terms of the recursive sequence $b_n,$ given by \[ b_{n+1} = 3b_{n} , \quad b_1 =2,\qquad n \in \mathbb{N}. \]

Let's write down the first few terms of our sequence: \begin{align*} b_1 & = 2 \\[10pt] b_2 &= 3b_1\\[5pt] &= 3(2)\\[5pt] &= 6 \\[10pt] b_3 &= 3b_2 \\[5pt] &= 3(6)\\[5pt] &=18 \\[10pt] b_4 &= 3b_3\\[5pt] &= 3(18)\\[5pt] &=54 \\[10pt] &\vdots \end{align*}

Therefore, \[ B = \left\{2,6,18,54, \ldots \right\}. \]

questions.question_html: For which values of $x$ is $x^2+6x \leq 0?$
questions.question_text: For which values of $x$ is $x^2+6x \leq 0?$
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's factor the left-hand side of the inequality: \begin{align*} x^2+6x&\leq 0 \\ x(x+6) &\leq 0 \end{align*}

Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $x(x+6)=0.$ The roots are $x=-6$ and $x=0,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ intervals $(-\infty,-6),$ $(-6,0),$ and $(0,\infty).$

Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:

$(-\infty,-6)$ $(-6,0)$ $(0,\infty)$

$x+6$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$ ${\color{blue}+}$

Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $x(x+6) \leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $x(x+6)$ is zero or negative.

We see that the expression is negative on the interval $(-6,0).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-6$ and $x=0.$

Therefore, we conclude that $x^2+6x \leq 0$ for all $x\in [-6,0].$

questions.question_text: For which values of $x$ is $2x^2-14x+12 \geq 0?$
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's factor the left-hand side of the inequality: \begin{align*} 2x^2-14x+12 &\geq 0 \\[3pt] 2(x^2-7x+6) &\geq 0 \\[3pt] 2(x-1)(x-6) &\geq 0 \end{align*}

Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $2(x-1)(x-6)=0.$ The roots are $x=1$ and $x=6,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ intervals $(-\infty,1),$ $(1,6),$ and $(6,\infty).$

Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:

$(-\infty,1)$ $(1,6)$ $(6,\infty)$

$x-6$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$

Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $2(x-1)(x-6) \geq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $2(x-1)(x-6)$ is zero or positive.

We see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\infty,1)$ and $(6,\infty).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=1$ and $x=6.$

Therefore, we conclude that $2x^2-14x+12 \geq 0$ for all $x\in (-\infty, 1]\cup[6, \infty).$

questions.question_html: For which values of $x$ is $3x^2-4x-30 > 5x+24?$
questions.question_text: For which values of $x$ is $3x^2-4x-30 > 5x+24?$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side: \begin{align*} 3x^2-4x-30 &> 5x+24\\[3pt] 3x^2-9x-54 &> 0\\[3pt] 3(x^2-3x-18) &> 0\\[3pt] 3(x+3)(x-6) &> 0 \end{align*}

Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $3(x+3)(x-6) = 0.$ The roots are $x=-3$ and $x=6,$ and they split the number line into $3$ subintervals $(-\infty, -3),$ $(-3,6),$ and $(6,\infty).$

Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:

$(-\infty,-3)$ $(-3,6)$ $(6,\infty)$
$x+3$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$ ${\color{blue}+}$
$x-6$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$
$3(x-6)(x+3)$ ${\color{blue}+}$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$

Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $3(x-6)(x+3) > 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $3(x-6)(x+3)$ is positive.

We see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\infty, -3)$ and $(6,\infty).$

Therefore, we conclude that $3x^2-4x-30 > 5x+24$ for all $x\in (-\infty, -3)\cup(6, \infty).$

questions.question_html: Find the solution of $x^2-x \geq 4-8x-x^2.$
questions.question_text: Find the solution of $x^2-x \geq 4-8x-x^2.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side: \begin{align*} x^2-x &\geq 4-8x-x^2 \\[5pt] 2x^2 + 7 x - 4 &\geq 0\\[5pt] % 2\left(x^2+\dfrac{7}{2}x-2\right)&\geq 0\\[5pt] %2(x+4)\left(x-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)&\geq 0\\[5pt] (x+4)(2x-1) & \geq 0 \end{align*}

Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+4)(2x-1).$ The roots are $x = -4$ and $x=\dfrac{1}{2},$ and they split the number line into the $3$ subintervals $(-\infty,-4),$ $\left(-4,\dfrac{1}{2}\right),$ and $\left(\dfrac{1}{2},\infty \right).$

Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:

$(-\infty,-4)$ $\left(-4,\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$ $\left(\dfrac{1}{2},\infty \right)$
$x+4$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$ ${\color{blue}+}$
$2x-1$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$
$(x+4)(2x-1)$ $({\color{red}-})\cdot({\color{red}-})={\color{blue}+}$ $({\color{blue}+})\cdot({\color{red}-})={\color{red}-}$ $({\color{blue}+})\cdot({\color{blue}+})={\color{blue}+}$

Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+4)(2x-1) \geq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+4)(2x-1)$ is zero or positive.

We see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\infty,-4)$ and $\left(\dfrac{1}{2}, \infty\right).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-4$ and $x=\dfrac{1}{2}.$

Therefore, we conclude that $x^2-x \geq 4-8x-x^2$ for all $x\in(-\infty,-4] \cup \left[\dfrac{1}{2}, \infty \right).$

questions.question_html: Find the solution of $\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\dfrac{1}{4}x \geq 3.$
questions.question_text: Find the solution of $\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\dfrac{1}{4}x \geq 3.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side: \begin{align*} \dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\dfrac{1}{4}x &\geq 3 \\[5pt] \dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\dfrac{1}{4}x -3 &\geq 0\\[5pt] \dfrac{1}{4}(x^2-x-12)&\geq 0\\[5pt] \dfrac{1}{4}(x+3)(x-4)&\geq 0\\[5pt] (x+3)(x-4)&\geq 0 \end{align*}

Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+3)(x-4).$ The roots are $x = -3$ and $x=4,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ subintervals $(-\infty,-3),$ $(-3,4),$ and $(4,\infty).$

Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:

$(-\infty,-3)$ $(-3,4)$ $(4,\infty)$
$x+3$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$ ${\color{blue}+}$
$x-4$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$
$(x+3)(x-4)$ $({\color{red}-})\cdot({\color{red}-})={\color{blue}+}$ $({\color{blue}+})\cdot({\color{red}-})={\color{red}-}$ $({\color{blue}+})\cdot({\color{blue}+})={\color{blue}+}$

Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+3)(x-4) \geq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+3)(x-4)$ is zero or positive.

We see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\infty,-3)$ and $(4, \infty).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-3$ and $x=4.$

Therefore, we conclude that $\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\dfrac{1}{4}x \geq 3$ for all $x\in(-\infty,-3] \cup [4, \infty).$

questions.topic_name: Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method
questions.example_title: Solving an Inequality by Rearranging Terms and Using the Sign Table Method
questions.question_html: For which values of $x$ is $4x^2-x-10 \leq 2+3x+3x^{2}?$
questions.question_text: For which values of $x$ is $4x^2-x-10 \leq 2+3x+3x^{2}?$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side: \begin{align*} 4x^2-x-10 &\leq 2+3x+3x^{2} \\[3pt] x^2-4x-12 &\leq 0 \\[3pt] (x+2)(x-6) &\leq 0 \end{align*}

Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+2)(x-6) = 0.$ The roots are $x = -2$ and $x = 6,$ and they split the number line into $3$ subintervals $(-\infty, -2),$ $(-2, 6)$ and $(6, \infty).$

Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:

$(-\infty,-2)$ $(-2,6)$ $(6,\infty)$
$x+2$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$ ${\color{blue}+}$
$x-6$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$
$(x-6)(x+2)$ ${\color{blue}+}$ ${\color{red}-}$ ${\color{blue}+}$

Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+2)(x-6) \leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+2)(x-6)$ is zero or negative.

We see that the expression is negative on the interval $(-2,6).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-2$ and $x=6.$

Therefore, we conclude that $4x^2-x-10 \leq 2+3x+3x^{2}$ for all $x \in [-2, 6].$

questions.topic_name: Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin
questions.example_title: Finding the Center and Radius of a Circle Given Its Equation
questions.question_html: What is the center and the radius of the circle with equation $x^2 + y^2 = 6 ?$
questions.question_text: What is the center and the radius of the circle with equation $x^2 + y^2 = 6 ?$
questions.explanation_html:
The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \[ x^2 + y^2 = r^2. \]

So, the equation $x^2 + y^2 =6$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius \begin{align*} r^2 &= 6 \\[5pt] r &= \sqrt{6} . \end{align*}

Note that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.

Therefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is $\sqrt{6}.$

questions.example_title: Finding the Equation of a Circle Given a Description
questions.question_html: Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $13$ units from the origin?
questions.question_text: Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $13$ units from the origin?
questions.explanation_html:
The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $13$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 13$ centered at the origin, as shown below.

So, the required equation is

\begin{align*} x^2 + y^2 &= r^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 &= 13^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 &= 169. \end{align*}

questions.example_title: Finding the Equation of a Circle Given Endpoints of a Diameter
questions.question_html: Given that the points $A$ and $B$ with coordinates $(6,5)$ and $(-6,-5),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?
questions.question_text: Given that the points $A$ and $B$ with coordinates $(6,5)$ and $(-6,-5),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?
questions.explanation_html:
The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is \[ x^2 + y^2 = r^2. \]

First, we calculate the length of the diameter $\overline{AB}\mathbin{:}$ \[ \begin{align*} AB&=\sqrt{{(5-(-5))}^2+{(6-(-6))}^2}\\[5pt] &=\sqrt{{10}^2+12^2}\\[5pt] &=\sqrt{244}\\[5pt] &=\sqrt{4\cdot 61}\\[5pt] &=2\sqrt{61} \end{align*} \]

Since $\overline{AB}$ is a diameter, the radius is \[ r = \dfrac{AB}{2}=\dfrac {2\sqrt{61}}{2}=\sqrt{61}. \]

So, the equation of the circle is

\begin{align*} x^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & = (\sqrt{61})^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & = 61. \end{align*}
questions.explanation_text:

AB&=\sqrt{{(5-(-5))}^2+{(6-(-6))}^2}\\[5pt]
&=\sqrt{{10}^2+12^2}\\[5pt]
&=\sqrt{244}\\[5pt]
&=\sqrt{4\cdot 61}\\[5pt]
&=2\sqrt{61}
\end{align*}
\]
Since $\overline{AB}$ is a diameter, the radius is
\[
r = \dfrac{AB}{2}=\dfrac {2\sqrt{61}}{2}=\sqrt{61}.
\]
So, the equation of the circle is
\begin{align*}
x^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\[5pt]
x^2 + y^2 & = (\sqrt{61})^2 \\[5pt]
x^2 + y^2 & = 61.
\end{align*}

So, the equation of the circle is \begin{align*} x^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & = {\left(\sqrt{37}\right)}^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & = 37. \end{align*}

First, we calculate the length of the diameter $\overline{AB}\mathbin{:}$
\[ \begin{align*}
AB&=\sqrt{{(6-(-6))}^2+{(1-(-1))}^2}\\[5pt]
&=\sqrt{{12}^2+2^2}\\[5pt]
&=\sqrt{148}\\[5pt]
&=\sqrt{4 \cdot 37}\\[5pt]
&=2\sqrt{37}
\end{align*}
\]
Since $\overline{AB}$ is a diameter, the radius is
\[r=\dfrac{AB}{2} =\dfrac {2\sqrt{37}}{2}=\sqrt{37}.\]
So, the equation of the circle is
\begin{align*}
x^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\[5pt]
x^2 + y^2 & = {\left(\sqrt{37}\right)}^2 \\[5pt]
x^2 + y^2 & = 37.
\end{align*}

questions.topic_name: Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin
questions.example_title: Finding the Equation of a Circle Given Endpoints of a Diameter
questions.question_html: Given that the points $X$ and $Y$ with coordinates $(3,3)$ and $(-3,-3),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?
questions.question_text: Given that the points $X$ and $Y$ with coordinates $(3,3)$ and $(-3,-3),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?
questions.explanation_html:
The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is \[ x^2 + y^2 = r^2. \]

First, we calculate the length of the diameter $\overline{XY}\mathbin{:}$

\begin{align*} XY &=\sqrt{{(3-(-3))}^2+{(3-(-3))}^2} \\[5pt] &=\sqrt{{6}^2+6^2}\\[5pt] &=\sqrt{36+36}\\[5pt] &=\sqrt{72}\\[5pt] &=\sqrt{2\cdot 36}\\[5pt] &=6\sqrt2 \end{align*}

Since $\overline{XY}$ is a diameter, the radius is

\[ r=\dfrac{XY}{2}=\dfrac {6\sqrt2}{2}=3\sqrt2. \]

So, the equation of the circle is

\begin{align*} x^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & = \left({3\sqrt2}\right)^2 \\[5pt] x^2 + y^2 & = 18. \end{align*}

questions.example_title: Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality Using the Graphical Method
questions.question_html: Solve the inequality $x^2 \gt 16$ using a graphical method.
questions.question_text: Solve the inequality $x^2 \gt 16$ using a graphical method.
questions.explanation_html:
Bringing all the terms to the left-hand side, we have \[ \eqalign{ x^2 & \gt 16 \\[3pt] x^2-16 & \gt 0. } \]

Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\, y = x^2-16 \, $ and find its roots: \[ \eqalign{ x^2-16 &= 0\\[3pt] x^2-4^2 &= 0\\[3pt] (x-4)(x+4) &= 0 } \] So the roots are $x= \pm 4.$

Solving the inequality $x^2-16 \gt 0$ is equivalent to finding the values of $x$ for which the parabola $y=x^2-16$ is above the $x$-axis (where ${\color{red}y \gt 0}$).

Since the leading coefficient of the quadratic expression is positive, the parabola opens upward. Therefore, the solution set lies outside the roots (but it does not include them), as shown below.

Therefore, the solution is $x \in \color{blue}(-\infty, -4) \cup (4, \infty).$

questions.question_html: Solve the inequality $x^2 \geq 4$ using a graphical method.
questions.question_text: Solve the inequality $x^2 \geq 4$ using a graphical method.
questions.explanation_html:
Bringing all the terms to the left-hand side, we have \[ \eqalign{ x^2 &\geq 4 \\ x^2-4 &\geq 0.} \]

Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\, y = x^2-4 \, $ and find its roots: \[ \eqalign{ x^2-4 &= 0\\ x^2-2^2 &= 0\\ (x-2)(x+2) &= 0 } \] So the roots are $x= \pm 2.$

Solving the inequality $x^2-4 \geq 0$ is equivalent to finding the values of $x$ for which the parabola $y=x^2-4$ is above or intersects the $x$-axis (where ${\color{red}y \geq 0}$).

Since the leading coefficient of the quadratic expression is positive, the parabola opens upward. Therefore, the solution set lies outside the roots (including them), as shown below.

Therefore, the solution is $x \in \color{blue}(-\infty, -2] \cup [2, \infty).$

questions.example_title: Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality Using the Absolute Value Method
questions.question_html: Solve the inequality $x^2-15 \lt 5$ using the absolute value method.
questions.question_text: Solve the inequality $x^2-15 \lt 5$ using the absolute value method.
questions.explanation_html:
Bringing the constant term to the right-hand side, we have \begin{align*} x^2 -15& \lt 5\\[3pt] x^2 & \lt 20. \end{align*}

Then, taking the square root of both sides of the inequality, we get

\[ \eqalign{ x^2 &\lt 20\\[3pt] \sqrt{x^2} &\lt \sqrt{20}\\[3pt] |x| & \lt 2\sqrt{5}. } \]

Now, using the definition of absolute value, we must have \[ -2\sqrt{5} \lt x \lt 2\sqrt{5}. \]

The solution is $x \in (-2\sqrt 5,2\sqrt 5).$

|x| & \lt 2\sqrt{5}.
}
\]

questions.example_title: Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality Using the Absolute Value Method
questions.question_html: Solve the inequality $x^2-1< -\sqrt{2}\,$ using the absolute value method.
questions.question_text: Solve the inequality $x^2-1< -\sqrt{2}\,$ using the absolute value method.
questions.explanation_html:
Bringing the constant $-1$ to the right-hand side, we have

\begin{align*} x^2-1&< -\sqrt{2} \\ x^2 &< 1-\sqrt{2}. \end{align*}

Notice that $1-\sqrt{2}< 0$ and that $x^2$, being a square, must be a positive number or zero.

Hence, $x^2$ cannot be less than $1-\sqrt{2}.$

Therefore, there is no solution.

questions.example_title: Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality by Manipulating the Inequality First
questions.question_text: Solve $6x^2< 9.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's simplify the inequality: \begin{align*} 6x^2 &< 9 \\ x^2 &< \dfrac{9}{6}\\ x^2 &< \dfrac{3}{2}\\ \end{align*}

Now, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.

Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Equation

Taking the square root of both sides of the inequality, we get \[ \eqalign{ \sqrt{x^2} &< \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\\ |x|&< \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}} .} \]

Now, using the definition of absolute value, we must have $-\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}< x< \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}.$

So, the solution is $x\in \left(-\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}},\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\right).$

Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve

Starting from $x^2 < \dfrac{3}{2}$ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach

\[ x^2-\dfrac 32 < 0 . \]

Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\, y = x^2-\dfrac{3}{2} \, $ and find its roots: \[ \eqalign{ x^2-\dfrac{3}{2} &= 0\\ x^2-\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\right)^2 &= 0\\ \left(x-\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\right)\left(x+\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\right) &= 0, } \] hence $x= \pm \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}.$

Since the leading coefficient of $x^2-\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}$ is positive, the parabola opens upward. Therefore, the solution set to the given inequality ($y < 0$) lies between the roots (but it does not include them):

Therefore, the solution is $x\in \left(-\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}},\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\right).$

questions.example_title: Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality by Manipulating the Inequality First
questions.question_text: Solve $4x^2> 9.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's simplify the inequality:

\begin{align*} 4x^2 &>9\\[3pt] x^2 &> \dfrac{9}{4}\\ \end{align*}

Now, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.

Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Equation

Taking the square root of both sides of the inequality, we get

\[ \eqalign{ \sqrt{x^2} &> \sqrt{ \dfrac{9}{4}}\\ |x| &> \dfrac{3}{2}.\\ } \]

Now, using the definition of absolute value, we must have

\[ x< -\dfrac{3}{2}\quad\textrm{or}\quad x>\dfrac{3}{2}. \]

So, the solution is $x \in \left(-\infty, -\dfrac{3}{2}\right) \cup\left ( \dfrac{3}{2}, +\infty\right).$

Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve

Starting from $x^2> \dfrac{9}{4} $ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach

\[ x^2- \dfrac{9}{4} > 0 . \]

Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\, y = x^2-\dfrac{9}{4} \, $ and find its roots: \[ \eqalign{ x^2-\dfrac{9}{4} &= 0\\ x^2-\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 &= 0\\ \left(x-\dfrac{3}{2}\right)\left(x+\dfrac{3}{2}\right) &= 0, } \] so the roots are $x= \pm \dfrac{3}{2}.$

Since the leading coefficient of $x^2-\dfrac{9}{4}$ is positive, the parabola opens upward. Hence, the solution set to the given inequality ($y > 0$) lies outside the roots and does not include them:

Therefore, the solution is $x \in \left(-\infty, -\dfrac{3}{2}\right) \cup \left(\dfrac{3}{2}, \infty\right).$

questions.example_title: Elementary Quadratic Inequalities with No Solutions or All Real Solutions
questions.question_html: Solve the inequality $5x^2+2x \gt 2x-3.$
questions.question_text: Solve the inequality $5x^2+2x \gt 2x-3.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's simplify the inequality:

\begin{align*} 5x^2+2x&> 2x-3\\[3pt] 5x^2+2x-2x &> 2x-3-2x\\[3pt] 5x^2&> -3. \end{align*}

Now, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.

Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality

We know that the square of any real number is greater than or equal to zero.

So, the inequality is true for every real number. Therefore the solution is $x \in (-\infty,\infty).$

Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve

Starting from $5x^2 \gt -3$ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach

\[ 5x^2+3 \gt 0. \]

Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\, y = 5x^2+3. $ :

Since the leading coefficient of $ 5x^2+3$ is positive, the parabola opens upward. The desired inequality $(y \gt 0)$ is always satisfied. Therefore, the solution set to the given inequality includes all real numbers. That is, $x \in (-\infty,\infty).$

questions.example_title: Elementary Quadratic Inequalities with No Solutions or All Real Solutions
questions.question_html: Solve the inequality $2x^2-10\leq 4x^2.$
questions.question_text: Solve the inequality $2x^2-10\leq 4x^2.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's simplify the inequality:

\begin{align*} 2x^2-10&\leq 4x^2 \\ 2x^2-4x^2 &\leq 10\\ -2x^2 &\leq 10\\ x^2&\geq -\dfrac{ 10}{2}\\ x^2&\geq -5. \end{align*}

Now, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.

Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality

We know that the square of any real number is greater than or equal to zero.

So, the inequality is true for every real number. Therefore the solution is $x \in (-\infty,\infty).$

Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve

Starting from $-2x^2 \leq 10$ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach

\[ -2x^2-10 \leq 0 . \]

Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $y = -2x^2-10$:

Since the leading coefficient of $ -2x^2-10$ is negative, the parabola opens downward (like $\frown$). The desired inequality $(y\leq 0)$ is always satisfied. Therefore, the solution set to the given inequality includes all real numbers. That is, $x \in (-\infty,\infty).$

questions.question_html: Solve the inequality $x^2< -7.$
questions.question_text: Solve the inequality $x^2< -7.$
questions.explanation_html:
We can solve this inequality using either of the two methods described below.

Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality

We know that the square of any real number is greater than or equal to zero. Therefore, it is impossible for the square of a real number to be smaller than $-7.$

So, the inequality $x^2 < -7$ has no solutions.

Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve

Let's bring all the terms to the left-hand side, \[ x^2+7 < 0, \] and then plot the parabola $y=x^2+7.$ The desired inequality ($y< 0$) is never satisfied, so there are no solutions.

questions.explanation_text:
We can solve this inequality using either of the two methods described below.
Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality
We know that the square of any real number is greater than or equal to zero. Therefore, it is impossible for the square of a real number to be smaller than $-2.$
So, the inequality $x^2 \lt -2$ has no solutions.
Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve
Let's bring all the terms to the left-hand side,
\[
x^2+ 2 < 0,
\]
and then plot the parabola $y=x^2+ 2.$ The desired inequality ($y< 0$) is never satisfied, so there are no solutions.

questions.question_html: Find the coordinates $(h,k)$ of the center of the circle above given that the points $(0,2)$ and $(8,4)$ lie on a diameter of the circle.
questions.question_text: Find the coordinates $(h,k)$ of the center of the circle above given that the points $(0,2)$ and $(8,4)$ lie on a diameter of the circle.
questions.explanation_html:

questions.example_title: Finding the Radius of a Circle on the Coordinate Plane Using the Distance Formula
questions.question_html: The points $A$ and $B$ have coordinates $(6,-4)$ and $(0,-2)$, respectively, and the segment $\overline{AB}$ is a diameter of a circle. Find the radius of the circle.
questions.question_text: The points $A$ and $B$ have coordinates $(6,-4)$ and $(0,-2)$, respectively, and the segment $\overline{AB}$ is a diameter of a circle. Find the radius of the circle.
questions.explanation_html:
The diameter can be calculated using the distance formula:

\begin{align*} d &= \sqrt{{(x_1 - x_2)}^2+{(y_1-y_2)}^2} \\[3pt] &= \sqrt{(6-0)^2 + (-4 - (-2))^2}\\[3pt] &= \sqrt{(6)^2+(-2)^2} \\[3pt] &= \sqrt{36 +4} \\[3pt] &= \sqrt{40}\\[3pt] &= 2\sqrt{10} \end{align*}

The radius is half the diameter: \[ r = \dfrac{d}{2} = 2\dfrac{\sqrt{10}}{2} = \sqrt{10} \]

questions.example_title: Determining the Center and Radius of a Circle on the Coordinate Plane
questions.question_html: Find the coordinates $(h,k)$ of the center of the circle above and its radius given that the points $(2,-3)$ and $(10,5)$ lie on a diameter of the circle.
questions.question_text: Find the coordinates $(h,k)$ of the center of the circle above and its radius given that the points $(2,-3)$ and $(10,5)$ lie on a diameter of the circle.
questions.explanation_html:
The center of the circle is located at the midpoint of the diameter. So, we can calculate the coordinates of the center using the midpoint formula:

\[ \eqalign{ (h,k) &= \left( \dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \dfrac {y_1+y_2}{2}\right) \\[5pt] &= \left(\dfrac{2+10}2,\dfrac{-3+5}2\right) \\[5pt] &= \left(\dfrac{12}2,\dfrac{2}2\right) \\[5pt] &= (6, 1). } \] The radius can be found by calculating the distance between the center $\left(6,1\right)$ and a point on the circle, such as $A(2,-3)\mathbin{:}$

\[ \eqalign{ r&=\sqrt{{(x_1-x_2)}^2+{(y_1-y_2)}^2}\\[3pt] &= \sqrt{(6 - 2)^2 + (1 - (-3))^2} \\[3pt] &= \sqrt{(4)^2+(4)^2} \\[3pt] &= \sqrt{16+16} \\[3pt] &= \sqrt{32}\\[3pt] &= 4\sqrt{2}. } \]
